http-request-cycle.title: HTTP Request Cycle
http-request-cycle.description: What happens between typing a URL and seeing a webpage

steps.heading: The client sends a request
steps.text: Your browser (the client) sends an HTTP request to a server. It includes the method (`GET`, `POST`, etc.), the URL, headers (content type, auth token), and an optional body for POST/PUT. Before the request reaches the server it passes through DNS resolution, TCP handshaking, and TLS negotiation.

steps.heading: DNS resolution
steps.text: DNS (Domain Name System) translates a human-readable domain like `api.example.com` into the IP address routers actually use to deliver packets. The lookup checks multiple caches in order — browser cache, OS cache, local resolver — before contacting upstream nameservers, so repeated requests for the same domain are nearly instant. A low DNS TTL enables fast failover during outages; a high TTL reduces lookup latency for stable services.

steps.heading: The server processes
steps.text: The server receives the request and passes it through your application code (Node.js, Python Flask, etc.) which determines what to do — validate authentication, apply business logic, and typically query a database. The server then assembles a response with the appropriate status code, headers, and body. The entire processing time from receiving the request to sending the response is often called "server latency" or "time to first byte" (TTFB).

steps.heading: Database query
steps.text: The application queries the database (PostgreSQL, SQLite, etc.) with SQL statements and waits for the results before continuing. Database queries are often the largest contributor to server response time — poorly written queries, missing indexes, or N+1 query patterns can slow responses significantly. Always use parameterized queries (`$1`, `$2` placeholders) to pass user-supplied values safely, preventing SQL injection.

steps.heading: Response sent back
steps.text: The server sends an HTTP response: a status code (200 OK, 404 Not Found), headers, and the body (`HTML`, `JSON`). The `Content-Type` header tells the browser how to parse the body. Caching headers like `Cache-Control` and `ETag` let the browser avoid redundant re-fetches for unchanged resources.

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Understanding the HTTP request cycle helps you debug network issues, read server logs, and design APIs. Every fetch() call in JavaScript follows this cycle.
commonMistakes: Forgetting that GET requests have no body — pass data via query string (?key=val) instead
Sending the wrong Content-Type header — if the server expects application/json but gets text/plain, the body will not parse correctly
Confusing authentication (401: who are you?) with authorization (403: you're not allowed) — they are different stages in the request lifecycle

http-rest.title: REST & HTTP Methods
http-rest.description: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE — the verbs of the web

steps.heading: REST — resources as URLs
steps.text: REST (Representational State Transfer) is a convention for designing APIs. Resources are represented as URLs, and HTTP methods (`GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`) express the action.

steps.heading: GET — retrieve data
steps.text: `GET` requests data from the server. It should be safe (no side effects) and idempotent (same result every time, no matter how often called). There is no request body — filters go in query parameters. Browsers, CDNs, and proxies can cache `GET` responses, making it the most performant verb.

steps.heading: POST — create a resource
steps.text: `POST` sends data to create a new resource. The server assigns the ID and returns the created resource with status `201 Created` and a `Location` header. `POST` is not idempotent — calling it twice creates two separate resources. Always protect POST endpoints with authentication.

steps.heading: PUT and PATCH
steps.text: `PUT` replaces the entire resource — omitted fields are set to null or removed. `PATCH` updates only the specified fields and leaves the rest unchanged. Both target a specific resource by ID in the URL. Use `PATCH` for partial updates in APIs to avoid accidentally wiping fields.

steps.heading: DELETE — remove a resource
steps.text: `DELETE` removes the specified resource identified by the URL. A successful delete typically returns `204 No Content` — meaning the operation succeeded and there is nothing to return. `DELETE` is idempotent: deleting an already-deleted resource should return `404`, but the caller's intent is satisfied either way. Always require authentication for delete operations to prevent unauthorized data loss.

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use REST conventions when building or consuming HTTP APIs. Consistent method + URL conventions make APIs intuitive and self-documenting.
commonMistakes: Using POST for everything instead of matching the HTTP method to the action (GET for reads, DELETE for deletes)
Putting actions in URLs like /deleteUser/42 — REST uses the HTTP method for the action and the URL for the resource
Returning 200 for created resources — use 201 Created for POST responses and 204 No Content for successful DELETEs

http-status.title: Status Codes
http-status.description: What 200, 404, and 500 mean — and why they matter

steps.heading: Every response has a status code
steps.text: HTTP status codes are 3-digit numbers that tell the client what happened with their request, without needing to parse the response body. The first digit groups them by category: 2xx success, 3xx redirection, 4xx client error, 5xx server error. Using the correct code makes APIs self-documenting and enables generic client behaviour — retry on 503, redirect on 301, show "not found" on 404.

steps.heading: 2xx — Success
steps.text: `200 OK` is the standard success response for `GET`, `PUT`, and `PATCH` requests that return a body. `201 Created` signals that a `POST` created a new resource — pair it with a `Location` header pointing to the new URL. `204 No Content` indicates success but no body to return, typically after `DELETE` or a write operation where echoing back the resource is unnecessary.

steps.heading: 3xx — Redirection
steps.text: `301 Moved Permanently` signals that a URL has changed forever — browsers and search engines cache this and use the new URL on all future visits. `302 Found` is a temporary redirect that clients follow but do not cache. `304 Not Modified` is used for caching: the server confirms the client's cached copy is still valid, so no response body needs to be transferred, saving bandwidth.

steps.heading: 4xx — Client errors
steps.text: 4xx errors signal that the client made a mistake — the request was wrong or the client lacks permission. `400 Bad Request` covers invalid or malformed input. `401 Unauthorized` means authentication is missing or invalid. `403 Forbidden` means authenticated but lacking permission. `404 Not Found` means the resource does not exist. `422 Unprocessable Entity` is preferred for semantic validation failures like a duplicate email.

steps.heading: 5xx — Server errors
steps.text: 5xx errors mean something went wrong on the server side — not the client's fault. 500 Internal Server Error is the general catch-all for unhandled exceptions. 503 Service Unavailable means the server is overloaded or down for maintenance — the client can retry later. Unlike 4xx errors, the client request was valid; the server simply could not fulfill it.

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Read status codes to understand what happened in an API call. Return the correct status from your own APIs to communicate intent clearly.
commonMistakes: Returning 200 for error responses — always use the correct status code (400 for bad input, 404 for missing resources)
Treating 401 and 403 as the same — 401 means not logged in, 403 means logged in but not permitted
Ignoring 5xx errors in the client — these are server failures that users need to know about, not silently swallow

http-ssr-csr.title: SSR vs CSR
http-ssr-csr.description: Server-Side Rendering vs Client-Side Rendering — how web pages are built and delivered.

steps.heading: Server-Side Rendering (SSR)
steps.text: In SSR, the server generates the complete HTML for each request and sends a ready-to-display page to the browser. The browser receives full markup immediately — minimal JavaScript needed to show content. Examples: PHP, Django templates, Ruby on Rails, Next.js with getServerSideProps. Pros: fast first paint, excellent SEO, works without JS. Cons: full server hit per navigation, slower subsequent transitions.

steps.heading: Client-Side Rendering (CSR)
steps.text: In CSR, the server sends a minimal HTML shell plus a JavaScript bundle. The browser downloads, parses, and executes the JS, which then renders the full UI. Examples: Create React App, Vue SPA, Angular — any single-page application. Pros: fast navigation after initial load, rich interactions, cheap static hosting. Cons: slow initial load (JS bundle must download+parse+execute), SEO requires extra configuration.

steps.heading: Direct Comparison
steps.text: SSR and CSR make different trade-offs. SSR wins on first paint speed and SEO but requires a server for every page view. CSR wins on subsequent navigation speed and hosting cost but struggles with initial load time and search engine crawling. Neither is universally better — choose based on your app's priorities.

steps.heading: Hybrid Approaches
steps.text: Modern meta-frameworks blend SSR and CSR per route. SSG (Static Site Generation) pre-builds HTML at build time — fastest possible, no server needed (Astro, Next.js). ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration) rebuilds pages on demand or on schedule. Islands Architecture uses SSR for the shell with selective CSR hydration for interactive parts. Next.js, Nuxt, and SvelteKit let you choose SSR/CSR/SSG per route.

steps.heading: React Server Components (RSC)
steps.text: RSC is a React-specific strategy introduced in React 19. Components can be either Server or Client. Server Components fetch data, render HTML, and send a compact JSON payload to the browser — no JavaScript is shipped for them. Client Components (marked 'use client') handle interactivity. RSC cuts bundle size dramatically and simplifies data fetching. Next.js 15 is the main framework implementing RSC today.

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use SSR for content that must be fresh per request and SEO-critical (product pages, news). Use CSR for dashboards and app UIs behind a login. Use SSG for marketing pages, docs, and blogs.
commonMistakes: Assuming CSR is always faster — for first-time visitors on slow connections, SSR often wins.
Using SSR for everything in Next.js — SSG pages are served from CDN and are much faster.
Ignoring hydration — React SSR sends HTML, then 'hydrates' it with JS; mismatches cause flicker.

db-intro.title: Databases & SQL Intro
db-intro.description: What databases are, why we use them, and how relational and NoSQL databases differ.

steps.heading: What is a Database?
steps.text: A database is an organised collection of structured data managed by a Database Management System (DBMS). Key components: the data itself, a schema (structure definition), the DBMS engine, queries, and users. Unlike plain files, databases support concurrent access, complex queries, referential integrity, and transactions.

steps.heading: Relational Databases
steps.text: Relational databases store data in tables — rows are records, columns are typed attributes. A primary key uniquely identifies each row (often SERIAL or AUTO_INCREMENT). ACID guarantees ensure data integrity: Atomicity (all-or-nothing), Consistency (always valid state), Isolation (concurrent-safe), Durability (committed = permanent). Examples: PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite.

steps.heading: NoSQL Databases
steps.text: NoSQL databases trade rigid schemas for flexibility and scale. Four main types: Document stores (MongoDB) — JSON-like documents with nested data. Key-Value (Redis) — ultra-fast in-memory, ideal for caching. Graph (Neo4j) — nodes and edges for relationship-heavy queries. Column-oriented (Cassandra) — high write throughput for time-series and analytics.

steps.heading: SQL vs NoSQL: When to Use Which
steps.text: Use SQL when: data has clear relationships, you need ACID guarantees, or you run complex JOIN queries and reports. Use NoSQL when: data structure is variable or nested, you need massive horizontal scale, or your workload is write-heavy. In practice, many systems use both — PostgreSQL for persistent data, Redis for caching.

steps.heading: SQL Syntax Primer
steps.text: SQL (Structured Query Language) is the standard for relational databases. CREATE TABLE defines a table's schema with column names, types, and constraints. Common data types: SERIAL (auto-increment integer), VARCHAR(n) (string up to n chars), INT, BOOLEAN, TIMESTAMP, TEXT (unlimited string), DECIMAL(p,s). Constraints: PRIMARY KEY, NOT NULL, UNIQUE, DEFAULT, FOREIGN KEY.

syntax.label: CREATE TABLE
syntax.note: Define schema with types and constraints

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use a relational database (PostgreSQL) when your data has relationships, you need ACID guarantees, or you run complex queries. Add a key-value store (Redis) for caching or sessions.
commonMistakes: Using TEXT for everything — choosing the right data type enables better validation and index performance.
Skipping NOT NULL — allow NULL only when absence of a value is genuinely meaningful.
Forgetting REFERENCES (foreign keys) — without them the database cannot enforce referential integrity.

postgres-queries.title: SQL Queries
postgres-queries.description: SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY — read data from PostgreSQL

steps.heading: Tables store rows of data
steps.text: A database table organises data as rows (individual records) and columns (fields with defined types) — similar to a spreadsheet but with strict type enforcement. Every table has a schema that defines column names and types upfront; the database rejects any row that violates those types or constraints. A `SERIAL PRIMARY KEY` column auto-increments so the database assigns unique IDs to each row automatically.

steps.heading: SELECT
steps.text: `SELECT` is the most-used SQL statement — it retrieves rows from one or more tables. `SELECT *` returns every column, which is convenient for exploration but wasteful in production since it transfers data you may not need. Always name the specific columns you need: `SELECT id, name, email FROM users` is faster, self-documenting, and insulates your code from future schema additions.

steps.heading: WHERE — filter rows
steps.text: `WHERE` filters rows before they are returned — only rows where the condition is true make it into the result set. Combine multiple conditions with `AND` (both must be true) and `OR` (either must be true), using parentheses to control precedence. Always use parameterized queries (`$1`, `$2`) rather than string interpolation to prevent SQL injection when condition values come from user input.

steps.heading: ORDER BY — sort results
steps.text: `ORDER BY` sorts the returned rows before delivery. `ASC` (ascending, smallest first) is the default; `DESC` reverses it. Sort by multiple columns to handle ties: `ORDER BY department ASC, salary DESC` sorts by department first and breaks ties by salary. Without `ORDER BY`, PostgreSQL makes no guarantee about row order — results that look sorted today may not be after a table vacuum.

steps.heading: LIMIT — control result count
steps.text: `LIMIT` caps how many rows are returned, which is essential for performance on large tables — without it a single query can return millions of rows and exhaust memory. Combined with `OFFSET`, it implements page-based pagination: page 1 is `LIMIT 10 OFFSET 0`, page 2 is `LIMIT 10 OFFSET 10`. For large datasets, keyset pagination (`WHERE id > last_seen_id`) is faster than OFFSET because it uses the index directly.

steps.heading: SELECT with WHERE and ORDER BY
steps.text: WHERE filters rows that match a condition. You can combine multiple conditions with AND and OR. ORDER BY sorts results — ASC (default) for ascending, DESC for descending. Always alias ambiguous columns in multi-table queries.

steps.heading: Aggregate Functions: COUNT, SUM, AVG
steps.text: Aggregate functions compute a single result from a set of rows. COUNT(*) counts rows, SUM adds numeric values, AVG computes the mean, MIN/MAX find extremes. They are typically combined with GROUP BY.

steps.heading: GROUP BY and HAVING
steps.text: GROUP BY collapses rows that share the same value in a column into a single row, letting aggregates operate per group. HAVING filters groups after aggregation — like WHERE, but for groups.

steps.heading: HAVING vs WHERE
steps.text: WHERE filters individual rows before GROUP BY runs. HAVING filters groups after GROUP BY — it can reference aggregate functions. Rule of thumb: if your condition uses COUNT, SUM, or AVG, use HAVING; otherwise use WHERE. Both can appear in the same query.

steps.heading: Subqueries
steps.text: A subquery is a SELECT nested inside another query. The inner query runs first; its result is used by the outer query. Scalar subqueries return a single value (usable in WHERE). IN subqueries return a list of values. Correlated subqueries reference the outer query's columns — powerful but run once per outer row, so prefer JOINs for performance on large datasets.

syntax.label: Parameterized query
syntax.note: Always use $1 params — never string interpolation

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use SELECT queries whenever you need to read data from PostgreSQL. Add WHERE to filter, ORDER BY to sort, LIMIT/OFFSET to paginate, and GROUP BY for aggregates.
commonMistakes: Using SELECT * in production — always name the columns you need to avoid fetching unnecessary data and to make schema changes safer
Forgetting LIMIT on large tables — without it a query can return millions of rows and crash your app
Using HAVING without GROUP BY — HAVING filters groups and only makes sense after GROUP BY; use WHERE to filter individual rows instead

postgres-joins.title: JOINs
postgres-joins.description: Combine data from multiple tables with INNER and LEFT JOIN

steps.heading: Data lives in separate tables
steps.text: In a relational database, data is split across tables to avoid duplication — each fact is stored in exactly one place. A `users` table stores user info; a `posts` table stores posts with a `user_id` column referencing users. This normalisation means updating a user's name in one place updates it everywhere automatically. JOINs reassemble this split data at query time when you need information from multiple tables together.

steps.heading: Foreign keys link tables
steps.text: A foreign key is a column that holds the primary key value of a related row in another table, creating a link between the two. Declaring it with `REFERENCES users(id)` tells PostgreSQL to enforce referential integrity — inserting a `user_id` that does not exist in `users` is rejected with a constraint violation. This server-side enforcement is more reliable than application-level checks because it protects data even from direct database access.

steps.heading: INNER JOIN
steps.text: `INNER JOIN` returns only rows where a match exists in both tables — the intersection. If a user has no posts, they are silently excluded from the result. For each match found, one output row is produced, so a user with three posts will appear on three output rows. Use `INNER JOIN` when both sides of the relationship must exist for the result to be meaningful.

steps.heading: LEFT JOIN
steps.text: `LEFT JOIN` returns all rows from the left (first) table, regardless of whether a matching row exists in the right table. Where no match is found, right-side columns are filled with `NULL`. This makes it ideal for "users and their orders, including users with zero orders" — a `LEFT JOIN` keeps everyone visible. Adding `WHERE right_table.id IS NULL` then finds rows with no match at all.

steps.heading: Aliases and multiple joins
steps.text: Table aliases (e.g. `FROM users u`) shorten repetitive qualified column names from `users.name` to `u.name`, keeping multi-join queries readable. You can chain multiple `JOIN`s to combine three or more tables in a single query — each `JOIN` adds another table and its `ON` condition. Indexing all foreign key columns used in `ON` clauses is critical; unindexed joins trigger full table scans and can be catastrophically slow on large datasets.

syntax.label: INNER JOIN
syntax.note: Only rows with matches in both tables

syntax.label: LEFT JOIN
syntax.note: All left rows, NULLs for missing right

syntax.label: Table aliases
syntax.note: Alias with short name for readability

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use JOINs when data you need lives in multiple tables linked by foreign keys. Use INNER JOIN when both sides must exist; use LEFT JOIN when you want results even if the right side is missing.
commonMistakes: Using INNER JOIN when LEFT JOIN is needed — if users with no orders should still appear, INNER JOIN will silently exclude them
Forgetting the ON clause — without it you get a Cartesian product (every row × every row), which can produce millions of rows
Not aliasing tables in multi-join queries — ambiguous column names cause errors; always alias when joining more than two tables

postgres-crud.title: Schema & CRUD
postgres-crud.description: Create tables, insert, update, and delete rows

steps.heading: CREATE TABLE
steps.text: `CREATE TABLE` defines the schema of a table — the column names, their data types, and any constraints that enforce data integrity. Choose types carefully: `TEXT` for strings, `INTEGER` for whole numbers, `TIMESTAMPTZ` for timestamps, and `DECIMAL` for money. Column constraints like `NOT NULL` and `UNIQUE` are enforced by the database engine on every insert and update, regardless of where the data comes from.

steps.heading: INSERT — add rows
steps.text: `INSERT INTO` adds one or more new rows to a table. Always list the column names explicitly so the query is resilient to future schema changes and self-documents what data it provides. Use `RETURNING id` (or `RETURNING *`) to get the auto-generated primary key and any server-set defaults back immediately, avoiding a second round-trip query to fetch the new row.

steps.heading: UPDATE — modify rows
steps.text: `UPDATE` modifies the values of existing rows. The `WHERE` clause determines which rows are affected — omitting it updates every single row in the table, which is one of the most common and destructive SQL mistakes. Always verify the `WHERE` clause before running an `UPDATE` in production, and use `RETURNING *` to confirm exactly which rows were changed.

steps.heading: DELETE — remove rows
steps.text: `DELETE FROM` permanently removes rows that match the `WHERE` condition. Like `UPDATE`, omitting `WHERE` deletes every row in the table — the structure remains but all data is gone with no built-in undo. For preserving history, consider a soft delete: add a `deleted_at` column and filter with `WHERE deleted_at IS NULL` rather than physically removing rows.

steps.heading: Constraints protect data
steps.text: Constraints enforce data integrity rules at the database level — they apply regardless of which application, migration script, or user connects. `PRIMARY KEY` gives each row a unique identifier. `NOT NULL` rejects missing values. `UNIQUE` prevents duplicates. `FOREIGN KEY` (`REFERENCES`) rejects values that reference non-existent rows in another table. Constraints catch data errors that application validation might miss.

syntax.label: INSERT with RETURNING
syntax.note: RETURNING gets the inserted row back

syntax.label: UPDATE with WHERE
syntax.note: Always WHERE — or every row updates

syntax.label: DELETE with WHERE
syntax.note: Always WHERE — or every row deletes

syntax.label: ON CONFLICT (upsert)
syntax.note: Insert or update if duplicate key

patterns.title: Full CRUD for a users table

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use these patterns for every table you create and every CRUD operation in your app. Always use RETURNING to get back the affected row and avoid a second query.
commonMistakes: Running UPDATE or DELETE without a WHERE clause — this modifies or removes every row in the table; always double-check your WHERE condition
Not using parameterized queries ($1, $2) — string interpolation in SQL is the most common cause of SQL injection vulnerabilities
Forgetting TIMESTAMPTZ vs TIMESTAMP — TIMESTAMP stores no timezone; TIMESTAMPTZ stores UTC and converts on read; always use TIMESTAMPTZ for created_at/updated_at

ts-basics.title: Basics
ts-basics.description: Static types that catch errors before your code runs

steps.heading: JavaScript types are implicit
steps.text: In JavaScript, variables have types at runtime but you never declare them — the engine infers the type from whatever value is assigned. This flexibility is convenient for small scripts but means type errors only surface when the code actually runs, often in production. TypeScript adds an optional static type layer that catches these mismatches before the code ever executes.

steps.heading: TypeScript makes types explicit
steps.text: TypeScript adds type annotations written after a colon — `let age: number = 25` tells the compiler the variable must always hold a number. Annotations are most valuable on function parameters and return types where TypeScript cannot always infer the type automatically. In practice, you omit annotations on local variables where TypeScript can infer the type from the assigned value, keeping code clean.

steps.heading: Type errors caught at compile time
steps.text: If you try to assign the wrong type, TypeScript flags it immediately with a red squiggle in your editor — before the code ever runs. The error message names the exact variable and describes the conflict: "Type 'string' is not assignable to type 'number'." This turns runtime surprises that would crash in production into compile-time errors you see while writing code.

steps.heading: Interfaces define object shapes
steps.text: An `interface` is a named description of an object shape — it lists the required properties and their types. Any object assigned that interface type must have all required properties with matching types; TypeScript errors if a property is missing or has the wrong type. Interfaces use structural typing: an object is compatible if it has at least all required properties, regardless of how it was created.

steps.heading: Generics — reusable typed functions
steps.text: Generics allow a function, class, or interface to work with any type while still preserving type information — unlike `any` which discards it. The type parameter `<T>` is a placeholder filled in at call time, usually inferred automatically from what you pass in. The key benefit: `first<T>(arr: T[]): T` guarantees the return type matches the array element type, so TypeScript knows `first([1,2,3])` returns a `number`.

steps.heading: Why TypeScript? A practical story
steps.text: JavaScript only fails at runtime — often in production. TypeScript moves mistakes to development time: the compiler blocks the build and highlights every broken call in your editor. Rename a field, change a function signature, pass the wrong type — TypeScript flags it instantly instead of at 2 AM.

syntax.label: Primitive annotations
syntax.note: any disables checks; unknown forces you to narrow first

syntax.label: Array type
syntax.note: Both syntaxes are equivalent; T[] is more common

syntax.label: Union type
syntax.note: Variable can hold any of the listed types

syntax.label: Type alias
syntax.note: Give a name to reusable types or literal unions

syntax.label: Function signature
syntax.note: Annotate parameters and the return type separately

syntax.label: void
syntax.note: Use void for functions that do not return a value

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Add TypeScript to any project that will grow beyond a few files, is worked on by a team, or needs to be maintained long-term. Start with strict: true in tsconfig to catch the most errors. Use unknown over any whenever a value comes from an external source.
commonMistakes: Overusing any — it silences all TypeScript errors but defeats the purpose; prefer unknown, a union type, or a proper interface
Annotating variables that TypeScript can already infer — let name: string = "Alice" is redundant; just write let name = "Alice"
Confusing type and interface — both can describe object shapes; interfaces are extensible with extends and are preferred for public APIs
Avoiding TypeScript because it feels verbose — the upfront type annotations save far more time than they cost when a refactor or bug would otherwise slip to production

webapi-dom.title: DOM API
webapi-dom.description: The Document Object Model represents your HTML as a tree of nodes. JavaScript can traverse, select, create, and modify elements.

steps.heading: DOM Tree Structure
steps.text: The DOM represents your HTML as a tree of nodes. The document object is the root. HTML elements are Element nodes; text inside them are Text nodes. JavaScript can traverse this tree using parent, child, and sibling relationships.

steps.heading: Selecting Elements
steps.text: querySelector returns the first element matching a CSS selector. querySelectorAll returns a NodeList of all matches. getElementById and getElementsByClassName are older alternatives — querySelector is more flexible and preferred.

steps.heading: Creating and Inserting Elements
steps.text: createElement(tag) creates a detached element. Set its properties (textContent, className, etc.) then attach it to the DOM with appendChild, prepend, or insertBefore. The element appears in the page only after attachment.

steps.heading: Reading and Changing Element Properties
steps.text: Use textContent to read or set text safely. classList provides add, remove, toggle, and contains for CSS class management. setAttribute / getAttribute read and write HTML attributes. style lets you set inline CSS directly.

steps.heading: DOM Interfaces
steps.text: The DOM is structured as a hierarchy of interfaces. Document is the entry point. Node is the base for all DOM nodes (elements, text, comments). Element extends Node and adds HTML-specific methods. Understanding this hierarchy explains which methods are available on which objects.

steps.heading: Advanced Querying
steps.text: Beyond querySelector, two traversal methods are especially useful: closest() walks up the ancestor chain and returns the first element matching a selector — perfect for event delegation. matches() checks whether an element matches a selector without traversing anywhere — useful in event handlers to filter targets.

steps.heading: NodeList vs Array
steps.text: querySelectorAll returns a NodeList, which looks like an array but lacks array methods (map, filter, reduce). Convert it with Array.from() or spread syntax. Also note: getElementsByClassName and getElementsByTagName return live HTMLCollections that update automatically when the DOM changes; querySelectorAll returns a static snapshot.

steps.heading: classList Advanced Methods
steps.text: `classList.toggle(class, force)` forces the class on (`true`) or off (`false`) without an `if/else`. `classList.replace('old', 'new')` swaps atomically. `.add()` and `.remove()` accept multiple class names.

steps.heading: data-* Attributes
steps.text: `data-*` attributes store custom data on HTML elements. Access via `el.dataset` — kebab-case converts to camelCase: `data-user-id` → `el.dataset.userId`. Writing to `dataset` sets the HTML attribute.

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use the DOM API to read, create, and modify page content from JavaScript without a framework.
commonMistakes: Running DOM queries before the document is ready — wrap in DOMContentLoaded or place script at end of body.
Using getElementById when the element has a class, not an id.
querySelectorAll returns a NodeList, not an Array — use Array.from() to use array methods on it.

webapi-fetch.title: Fetch API
webapi-fetch.description: Make HTTP requests from JavaScript with Promises and async/await

steps.heading: The Fetch API
steps.text: `fetch()` is the modern browser API for making HTTP requests — it replaced the verbose `XMLHttpRequest` API with a clean Promise-based interface. It sends the request and returns a `Promise<Response>` that resolves when the server replies, while your other JavaScript continues running unblocked. The same API works in all modern browsers and in Node.js 18+ natively, with no library required.

steps.heading: fetch() returns a Promise
steps.text: The request is sent immediately but the response arrives asynchronously. `fetch()` returns a `Promise` that resolves to a `Response` object when the server replies. This two-stage process — first the Promise for the response headers, then another `.json()` or `.text()` call to read the body — allows inspecting status codes before downloading the full body, which is useful for streaming large responses.

steps.heading: The Response object
steps.text: The `Response` object contains everything the server sent back: `status` (HTTP code like 200 or 404), `ok` (true for 2xx), `headers`, and the body as a readable stream. The body must be explicitly parsed with `.json()`, `.text()`, or `.blob()` — calling the wrong one or calling it twice on the same response throws an error. The most important check: `response.ok` being false means success resolved but the request failed (e.g. 404 or 500).

steps.heading: Parsing the response body
steps.text: `response.json()` reads the entire response body and parses it as JSON, returning another `Promise` — that is why you need two `await` calls in a typical fetch pattern. `response.text()` returns the raw string, and `response.blob()` returns binary data for images or files. The body can only be consumed once; calling `.json()` after `.text()` on the same response throws because the stream is already exhausted.

steps.heading: async/await syntax
steps.text: `async`/`await` makes fetch code read like sequential, synchronous code rather than nested `.then()` chains. Always wrap in `try`/`catch` to handle network failures. One important gotcha: `fetch()` only rejects (throws) on network errors — HTTP errors like 404 and 500 still resolve successfully with `ok: false`, so always check `if (!res.ok) throw new Error(...)` before reading the body.

steps.heading: GET — Reading a Response
steps.text: fetch() returns a Promise that resolves to a Response object. Call response.json() to parse the body as JSON (also async). Always check response.ok or response.status before trusting the data.

steps.heading: POST / PUT / DELETE with a Body
steps.text: To send data, pass an options object as the second argument to `fetch`. Set the `method`, a `Content-Type: application/json` header, and serialize the body to JSON with `JSON.stringify`. Without the `Content-Type` header, many servers will not know how to parse the request body.

steps.heading: RESTful API Principles
steps.text: REST uses HTTP methods semantically: GET reads, POST creates, PUT/PATCH updates, DELETE removes. Each endpoint represents a resource (e.g. /posts, /posts/1). Responses carry HTTP status codes — 2xx for success, 4xx for client errors, 5xx for server errors.

steps.heading: Error Handling Patterns
steps.text: `fetch()` only rejects on **network failure**. A `404` or `500` still resolves — `res.ok` is `false`. Use `try/catch` for network errors and `if (!res.ok) throw` for HTTP errors.

steps.heading: AbortController
steps.text: Pass `{ signal: controller.signal }` to `fetch()`. Call `controller.abort()` to cancel. In React `useEffect`, `abort()` in the cleanup function prevents state updates after unmount.

syntax.label: POST with body + headers
syntax.note: Always set Content-Type for JSON bodies

syntax.label: Response body methods
syntax.note: Body can only be consumed once

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use fetch() to communicate with backend APIs from the browser. Prefer async/await over .then() chains for readability.
commonMistakes: Not checking res.ok — fetch() only rejects on network failure, not on 4xx/5xx responses; always check res.ok or res.status
Forgetting to await res.json() — the body parse is also async; calling .json() without await returns a Promise, not the data
Not setting Content-Type: application/json when posting JSON — the server may not parse the body correctly without this header

webapi-events.title: DOM Events
webapi-events.description: Respond to user interactions with event listeners

steps.heading: Events are user actions
steps.text: The browser fires events whenever users interact — clicking buttons, typing in fields, scrolling, hovering, submitting forms. JavaScript registers listener functions that are called automatically when a matching event occurs, without needing to constantly poll for changes. Events cover user input, browser lifecycle (page load, before unload), network status, and more.

steps.heading: addEventListener
steps.text: `addEventListener(type, handler)` attaches a callback that runs every time the specified event fires on that element. Multiple listeners can be added to the same element and event type — they all fire independently in the order added. To remove a listener later with `removeEventListener`, you must pass the same function reference that was originally added; inline arrow functions cannot be removed because each creates a new object.

steps.heading: The Event object
steps.text: Every event listener receives an `Event` object describing what happened: `type` (the event name), `target` (the element the event originated on), and event-specific properties. `MouseEvent` adds `clientX`/`clientY` for mouse position; `KeyboardEvent` adds `key` and modifier flags. Note that `target` (where the event originated) can differ from `currentTarget` (where the listener is attached) when using event delegation.

steps.heading: Event bubbling
steps.text: Events bubble up the DOM tree by default — a click on a `<button>` also fires on the parent `<div>`, then `<body>`, then `document`. This enables event delegation: instead of attaching listeners to every child, attach one listener to a parent and use `event.target` to identify which child was clicked. Delegation is more efficient and automatically covers dynamically added children. Call `stopPropagation()` when a nested element should handle the event exclusively.

steps.heading: stopPropagation and preventDefault
steps.text: `stopPropagation()` stops the event from bubbling up through the DOM so parent handlers don't also fire. `preventDefault()` cancels the browser's built-in action — preventing navigation on a link click or page reload on a form submit. The two methods are independent: you can call one, both, or neither depending on what you need.

steps.heading: EventTarget and addEventListener
steps.text: Any DOM element implements the EventTarget interface, giving it addEventListener(type, handler, options). You can attach multiple listeners to the same event type. Always use addEventListener instead of inline handlers for better separation of concerns.

steps.heading: Event Propagation: Bubbling and Capturing
steps.text: Events first travel down the DOM tree from root to target (capture phase), then bubble back up from target to root (bubble phase). By default, listeners run in the bubble phase. Call event.stopPropagation() to prevent the event from traveling further.

steps.heading: Custom Events
steps.text: `new CustomEvent(name, { detail })` creates a custom event. `element.dispatchEvent(event)` fires it. Set `bubbles: true` so parent elements can catch it. Any ancestor with `addEventListener(name, handler)` receives it.

steps.heading: Event Delegation
steps.text: Attach one listener to the parent instead of N listeners to children. Use `event.target.closest(selector)` to identify which child triggered the event. Works for elements added after the listener is attached.

syntax.label: addEventListener
syntax.note: Attach a listener to an element

syntax.label: removeEventListener
syntax.note: Inline arrow functions cannot be removed

syntax.label: Common event names
syntax.note: Full list at MDN Event reference

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use DOM events to respond to user interactions: clicks, form input, keyboard shortcuts, and scroll. In React, use synthetic events on JSX elements instead of addEventListener directly.
commonMistakes: Using an inline arrow function with removeEventListener — it creates a new function reference each time, so the listener is never actually removed
Forgetting preventDefault on form submit — without it the browser navigates to the form action URL and reloads the page
Not cleaning up event listeners on unmount — listeners on window or document persist after the component unmounts, causing memory leaks and duplicate handlers

webapi-storage.title: localStorage
webapi-storage.description: Persist data in the browser without a server

steps.heading: Browser storage
steps.text: Browsers provide built-in key-value storage that persists data locally without any server involvement. `localStorage` retains data indefinitely across browser restarts; `sessionStorage` is cleared when the tab closes. Both are scoped to the origin (protocol + domain + port) and ideal for storing user preferences, cached responses, and session state that should survive page refreshes.

steps.heading: setItem
steps.text: `setItem(key, value)` stores the value under the given key — both must be strings. Non-string values are coerced, so objects become `"[object Object]"` and are unreadable on retrieval. Always serialize complex values with `JSON.stringify` before storing, and deserialize with `JSON.parse` when reading. Namespace your keys (e.g. `"myapp:theme"`) to avoid collisions with other scripts sharing the same storage.

steps.heading: getItem
steps.text: `getItem(key)` returns the stored string value, or `null` if the key has never been set or was removed. Always check for `null` before parsing: passing `null` to `JSON.parse` will throw a `SyntaxError`. A safe pattern is `JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem("key") ?? "{}")` — the nullish coalescing operator provides a valid JSON fallback when the key is absent.

steps.heading: localStorage persists
steps.text: Data in `localStorage` persists permanently — it survives page refreshes, tab closes, and full browser restarts until explicitly cleared with `removeItem(key)` or `clear()`. This makes it suitable for long-lived preferences like theme, language, or user settings. A useful side effect: the `storage` event fires on other open tabs when `localStorage` changes, enabling cross-tab state synchronisation.

steps.heading: sessionStorage — tab-scoped
steps.text: `sessionStorage` has the exact same `setItem`/`getItem`/`removeItem` API as `localStorage`, but data is cleared automatically when the tab or window closes. Each tab gets its own completely isolated `sessionStorage` — two tabs on the same site cannot share it. Use `sessionStorage` for temporary UI state like multi-step form progress or wizard steps that should reset when the user is done.

steps.heading: localStorage vs sessionStorage
steps.text: Both store key/value string pairs in the browser, but localStorage persists across browser sessions while sessionStorage is cleared when the tab closes. Neither should store sensitive data — they are accessible to any script on the page.

steps.heading: setItem / getItem / removeItem / clear
steps.text: The Web Storage API is a simple key/value store. All values are stored as strings — use `JSON.stringify` and `JSON.parse` to store and retrieve objects. The API is synchronous and blocks the main thread, so avoid storing large data sets; use IndexedDB for anything beyond small config or preferences.

steps.heading: Safe Read with Fallback
steps.text: Wrap `localStorage.getItem` + `JSON.parse` in `try/catch`. Return a typed fallback if the key is `null` or the JSON is invalid. Prevents crashes from corrupted or missing data.

steps.heading: What NOT to Store
steps.text: Never store passwords, auth tokens, or session IDs in `localStorage`. Any XSS vulnerability exposes all storage. Use `httpOnly` cookies for auth — they are inaccessible to JavaScript by design.

syntax.label: removeItem / clear
syntax.note: clear() wipes everything in the origin

syntax.label: JSON.stringify / parse
syntax.note: Objects must be serialized to string

syntax.label: Null check on getItem
syntax.note: getItem returns null if key missing

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use localStorage for user preferences, cached data, or state that should survive page reloads. Use sessionStorage for temporary state within a single session. Never store sensitive data like passwords or tokens.
commonMistakes: Storing objects directly without JSON.stringify — localStorage only stores strings; objects become "[object Object]"
Not handling the null return from getItem — if the key was never set getItem returns null, which causes JSON.parse(null) errors
Storing auth tokens in localStorage — it is accessible via JavaScript and vulnerable to XSS; use HttpOnly cookies for sensitive tokens instead

react-intro.title: React — The Big Picture
react-intro.description: Where React came from, how it evolved, and how to start a modern React project

steps.heading: 2013 — JSX & the component idea
steps.text: React was open-sourced by Facebook in 2013. Its core insight: build UIs from small, reusable pieces called components. JSX — an HTML-like syntax inside JavaScript — was created alongside it. React could run on a server from day one, but it was mainly used to build interactive SPAs in the browser.

steps.heading: 2015 — React everywhere
steps.text: React Native launched in 2015, bringing the same component model to iOS and Android native apps. Major companies — Netflix, Airbnb, Dropbox — adopted React. ES6 class syntax became the standard way to write stateful components with lifecycle methods (componentDidMount, componentDidUpdate, componentWillUnmount).

steps.heading: 2019 — Hooks change everything
steps.text: React 16.8 introduced Hooks — useState, useEffect, and friends. Hooks let functional components manage state and side effects without class syntax. They quickly became the de facto standard. Code became shorter, easier to compose, and simpler to test. Class components still work but are rarely written in new projects.

steps.heading: 2024 — Back to the server
steps.text: React 19 introduced Server Components. Components can now run on the server, fetch data directly from a database, and stream HTML + JSON to the client. Only "Client Components" (marked with "use client") ship JavaScript to the browser. Frameworks like Next.js 15 and Remix/React Router v7 are the primary way to use Server Components today.

steps.heading: Starting a React project today
steps.text:
Two strategies: use a meta-framework (recommended for production) or assemble your own tooling.

Meta-frameworks handle routing, data fetching, bundling, and rendering strategy for you:
• Next.js 15 — full-stack web apps, file-system routing, Server Components
• Remix / React Router v7 — nested routes, server mutations, progressive enhancement
• Expo SDK 52+ — iOS/Android native apps with React Native

DIY tooling gives you full control and teaches fundamentals:
• Vite (used in this course) — instant dev server, Rollup-powered builds
• Parcel — zero-config bundler
• Rsbuild + Rspack — Rust-powered, 5–10× faster than Webpack

syntax.label: Vite + React scaffold
syntax.note: Used in this course — fast, minimal, Rollup-based

syntax.label: Server Component (Next.js)
syntax.note: No "use client" = runs on server by default

syntax.label: Client Component
syntax.note: "use client" makes it interactive, ships JS to browser

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use Vite when learning React core APIs or building SPAs. Reach for Next.js when you need server rendering, API routes, or full-stack capabilities. Use Expo for mobile.
commonMistakes: Starting with a meta-framework before understanding React basics — you'll confuse React's own APIs with framework magic
Using Create React App (CRA) — it is deprecated and unmaintained; use Vite instead
Writing class components in new projects — Hooks are the modern standard; class components are only needed for error boundaries

react-components.title: Components & JSX
react-components.description: Build UIs from reusable, composable function components

steps.heading: HTML is static markup
steps.text: Plain HTML defines the structure once. To reuse a button, you copy-paste the markup — which quickly becomes hard to maintain. If the button style changes, every copy has to be updated manually. React solves this by letting you define a component once and use it anywhere.

steps.heading: JSX — HTML in JavaScript
steps.text: `JSX` lets you write HTML-like syntax inside JavaScript. It looks like HTML but is transformed into JavaScript function calls by a build tool like Vite or Babel. This means you can use the full power of JavaScript — variables, conditionals, loops — directly inside your markup.

steps.heading: A component is a function
steps.text: A React component is a function that accepts `props` and returns `JSX`. The function name must start with a capital letter so React can distinguish it from a plain HTML tag. You define a component once and render it as many times as needed — each instance is independent.

steps.heading: Components compose
steps.text: You build complex UIs by nesting components inside one another — forming a component tree. The top-level component (usually `App`) is the root; everything else branches from it. Each component only knows about its direct children, which keeps the codebase modular and easy to navigate.

steps.heading: Props — data flows down
steps.text: `props` are the arguments you pass to a component, just like function arguments. They flow only from parent to child — never the other way — which makes data flow predictable. A component should treat its props as read-only and never modify them directly.

steps.heading: Events
steps.text: React uses camelCase event props (`onClick`, `onChange`, `onSubmit`). You pass a function reference — not a call. React wraps the native event in a SyntheticEvent that works the same across all browsers. For text inputs, `onChange` is the key to controlled components: bind `value` to state and update it on every keystroke. Always initialize text input state with an empty string (`useState('')`) — starting with `null` or `undefined` causes React to warn about switching from uncontrolled to controlled. For checkboxes, read `event.target.checked`, not `event.target.value`.

steps.heading: Conditional Rendering
steps.text: Use plain JavaScript inside JSX to conditionally show UI. Ternary for either/or, `&&` for show-or-nothing, and early return for complex branches. Watch out: if the condition is `0`, the `&&` operator renders the number `0` in the DOM rather than nothing — use `!!count` to coerce to boolean.

steps.heading: Rendering Lists
steps.text: Use `.map()` to turn an array into JSX elements. Every sibling in a list needs a stable `key` prop — React uses it to track which items were added, removed, or reordered. Use a unique ID from your data, never the array index. Generate IDs when the data is created — not during render — so keys stay stable across re-renders; generating them inside `.map()` assigns a new key on every render, making React treat every item as brand new.

syntax.label: JSX conditional (&&, ternary)
syntax.note: && renders nothing when false; ternary for either/or

syntax.label: .map with key
syntax.note: key must be stable and unique among siblings

syntax.label: children prop
syntax.note: children is whatever is between the tags

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use function components for all new React code. Define a TypeScript interface for props whenever a component accepts more than one or two props.
commonMistakes: Using index as key in lists — if items reorder or are removed, index keys cause React to misidentify elements and produce rendering bugs
Mutating props inside a component — props are read-only; if you need to change a value, copy it into local state with useState
Calling a component as a plain function — always render as JSX (<MyComp />) not as a function call (MyComp()), otherwise React cannot track its lifecycle

react-state.title: State & Hooks
react-state.description: Manage dynamic data with useState and understand re-renders

steps.heading: State is dynamic data
steps.text: State is data that can change over time and causes the component to re-render when it does. Props come from outside; state lives inside the component. Without state, variables reset to their initial value on every render because React calls the component function fresh each time. The `useState` hook stores values in a persistent slot so they survive re-renders.

steps.heading: useState
steps.text: `useState` is a Hook that returns a pair: the current value and a setter function. React stores the value outside the component so it survives re-renders. Call the setter to update the value — React will re-render the component with the new state on the next cycle.

steps.heading: Calling the setter re-renders
steps.text: When you call `setCount`, React schedules a re-render — it does not update the variable immediately. The component function runs again from top to bottom with the new state value, and every child component re-renders as well. React then compares the new output to the previous snapshot and updates only what actually changed in the DOM. State updates within the same event handler are batched together so React only re-renders once per event.

steps.heading: State is local to each instance
steps.text: If you render two `<Counter />` components, each has its own independent count — state is local to the component instance, not to the component type. State is not shared unless you lift it up to a common parent and pass it down as props. This is the fundamental mechanism for coordinating state between sibling components in React.

steps.heading: Rules of Hooks
steps.text: Hooks must always be called at the top level of a component — never inside conditions, loops, or nested functions. React relies on the order of Hook calls to associate state with the right variable. Breaking this rule causes bugs that are difficult to track down. ESLint's `eslint-plugin-react-hooks` enforces this automatically.

steps.heading: Updater functions
steps.text: When the new state depends on the previous value, pass a function to the setter instead of a value. React calls it with the most recent state — even if multiple updates were batched.

steps.heading: State with objects & arrays
steps.text: Never mutate state directly — React compares state by reference, not by value. Calling `.push()` on an array or changing a property on an object keeps the same reference, so React sees no change and skips the re-render entirely. Always create a new array or object: use spread to copy and override for objects (`{...user, email: nextEmail}`), and `.map()`, `.filter()`, or spread for arrays.

steps.heading: Lifting state up
steps.text: Siblings cannot share state directly. Lift state to their closest common parent. The parent owns the state and passes it down via props — along with the setter so children can trigger updates.

syntax.label: Functional updater
syntax.note: Use prev => when new state depends on old state

syntax.label: Object state spread update
syntax.note: Never mutate the state object directly

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use useState for any value that changes over time and should trigger a re-render: form inputs, toggle state, loaded data, counters.
commonMistakes: Mutating state directly — doing state.push(item) or state.name = "x" does not trigger a re-render; always call the setter with a new value
Not using the functional updater when new state depends on old state — setCount(count + 1) can miss updates in batched renders; use setCount(prev => prev + 1)
Storing derived values in state — if a value can be computed from existing state or props, compute it during render instead of keeping a separate useState

react-useeffect.title: useEffect & Side Effects
react-useeffect.description: Run code after render — fetch data, subscribe, sync with the DOM

steps.heading: Side effects happen after render
steps.text: A side effect is any work that affects something outside the component: network requests, timers, DOM manipulation, event subscriptions. `useEffect` runs these after the render so the render function stays pure. Running side effects directly in the render body causes them to fire on every re-render, including those triggered by unrelated state changes.

steps.heading: Empty deps — run once
steps.text: Passing an empty array `[]` as the second argument tells React to run the effect only once — when the component first mounts. Without a dependency array the effect runs after every render, which is almost never what you want. The array signals to React which values the effect depends on; an empty array means it depends on nothing and therefore never needs to re-run.

steps.heading: Deps array — re-run on change
steps.text: List the values the effect depends on in the second argument. React re-runs the effect whenever any of those values change between renders. If you reference a variable inside the effect but omit it from the array, you risk the effect reading a stale value — ESLint's exhaustive-deps rule catches this.

steps.heading: Cleanup function
steps.text: Return a function from `useEffect` to clean up before the next run or on unmount. Essential for `setInterval`/`clearInterval`, subscriptions, and event listeners. Without cleanup, an unmounted component that still has an active subscription will trigger React's "state update on unmounted component" warning.

steps.heading: Common use cases
steps.text: `useEffect` is for anything that needs to happen outside the render cycle: data fetching, subscriptions, DOM manipulation, analytics. Each use case typically needs both the setup and the corresponding cleanup — fetch requests should be aborted, intervals cleared, event listeners removed. Getting cleanup right is what separates a correct effect from a leaky one.

steps.heading: Fetching & race conditions
steps.text: When fetching inside useEffect, a fast second request can resolve before a slow first one — causing stale data to overwrite the correct result. The classic fix is an `ignore` flag: initialize it as `false`, set it to `true` in the cleanup function, and check it before updating state so earlier in-flight responses are discarded. Alternatively, pass an `AbortController` signal to `fetch` to cancel the in-flight request outright when the dependency changes or the component unmounts.

steps.heading: When NOT to use useEffect
steps.text: useEffect is for syncing with external systems. Avoid it for: (1) derived values — compute them during render instead; (2) event-triggered side effects — put them in the handler; (3) chained state updates — they cause double renders.

syntax.label: Run on dep change ([dep])
syntax.note: List all values the effect reads

syntax.label: Cleanup return
syntax.note: Returned function runs before next effect + on unmount

syntax.label: Run every render (no array)
syntax.note: Omit second arg to run after every render

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use useEffect for side effects that must happen after render: data fetching, subscriptions, timers, and DOM manipulation. If the effect reads state or props, list them in the deps array.
commonMistakes: Missing dependencies in the deps array — if the effect uses a prop or state value, it must be in the array or the effect will run with a stale closure
Forgetting the cleanup return — effects that set up subscriptions, timers, or event listeners must return a cleanup function to prevent memory leaks
Putting an async function directly in useEffect — useEffect cannot return a Promise; wrap async code in an inner async function and call it

react-router.title: React Router
react-router.description: Add client-side navigation to your app — pages, links, and URL parameters

steps.heading: Client-side routing
steps.text: Traditional websites load a new HTML page on every navigation. React Router intercepts the URL change and swaps components instead — no full page reload. This preserves JavaScript state in memory and makes transitions feel instant because only the changed components re-render. The browser History API updates the URL bar so the back button and bookmarks still work as expected.

steps.heading: BrowserRouter wraps your app
steps.text: `BrowserRouter` provides routing context to your entire app — it must wrap the root of your component tree exactly once. It uses the HTML5 History API to keep the URL in sync with the UI. In production, the server must be configured to serve `index.html` for all paths, or a hard refresh on any route other than `/` will return a 404.

steps.heading: Routes and Route
steps.text: `Routes` scans its children and renders the first `Route` whose `path` matches the current URL. Each `Route` maps a path pattern to a component via the `element` prop. Wrapping all routes inside a single `<Routes>` ensures only one route is active at a time.

steps.heading: Link — navigate without reload
steps.text: Use `Link` instead of `<a href>` for in-app navigation. `Link` updates the URL and swaps the matched component without a full page reload — preserving JavaScript state and avoiding a network round-trip. The `to` prop accepts both static strings and dynamic expressions.

steps.heading: useParams — dynamic segments
steps.text: URL segments prefixed with `:` are dynamic — they match any value in that position. `useParams` returns an object containing the matched values, keyed by the segment name. Use them to load the right data for a specific user, product, or post based on the URL.

steps.heading: Layout routes & Outlet
steps.text: Nest child routes inside a parent Route that renders shared UI (navbar, sidebar). The parent component uses <Outlet /> as a placeholder — React Router renders the matched child route there.

steps.heading: Index routes & useNavigate
steps.text: An index route is the default child — it renders when the parent path matches exactly. useNavigate lets you navigate programmatically: after form submission, login, or any event.

syntax.label: Link
syntax.note: Use Link instead of <a> to avoid full reload

syntax.label: Outlet (nested routes)
syntax.note: Outlet is where child routes render

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use React Router when your app has multiple views that map to different URLs. Use Link for navigation in the UI and useNavigate for programmatic navigation after events like form submission.
commonMistakes: Using <a href> instead of <Link> — anchor tags cause a full page reload and lose React state; always use Link for internal navigation
Forgetting to add BrowserRouter at the app root — all Router hooks (useNavigate, useParams, etc.) throw errors if there is no Router context above them
Assuming useParams values are numbers — URL params are always strings; parse with parseInt or Number() before using as a numeric ID

react-context.title: Context & useReducer
react-context.description: Share state across the component tree without prop drilling — and manage complex state with useReducer

steps.heading: Prop drilling
steps.text: Prop drilling is passing data through intermediate components that don't use it — just so a deeply nested child can receive it. As trees grow, every layer must declare and forward props it doesn't care about.

steps.heading: createContext & Provider
steps.text: createContext() creates the context object. Wrap the subtree that needs access with <XContext.Provider value={...}>. Every component inside can read that value directly — no matter how deep.

steps.heading: useContext
steps.text: useContext(MyContext) reads the nearest Provider above and returns its value. The component re-renders automatically when the value changes. Any component can consume — no prop passing required.

steps.heading: When to use Context
steps.text: Context works best for data that is truly global or shared across many components — like the current user, theme, or language. Avoid it for frequently-changing values: every context change re-renders all consumers, even those that only use a small part of the data. When your context value is an object, wrap it in `useMemo` so a new reference is not created on every parent render.

steps.heading: useReducer — what & why
steps.text: When useState manages multiple interrelated fields — like a cart with items, total, and count — every update must touch all of them consistently. useReducer centralises all transitions into one pure function: (state, action) => newState.

steps.heading: dispatch & actions
steps.text: useReducer returns [state, dispatch]. Actions are plain objects with a type string and optional payload. The reducer switches on type and returns a new state object — never mutate existing state directly.

steps.heading: Reducer + Context together
steps.text: Combine both patterns: put useReducer inside a Provider and expose [state, dispatch] through context. Any child can read state or fire actions without prop drilling. A custom hook gives consumers a clean, error-safe API.

ts-interfaces.title: Interfaces
ts-interfaces.description: Define object contracts — optional props, readonly fields, extension, and implementation

steps.heading: Interfaces describe object shapes
steps.text: An interface defines a contract: any object assigned that type must have exactly those properties with those types. It documents intent and lets TypeScript enforce it. TypeScript uses structural typing — it checks the shape of the value, not which class it comes from, so any object with the right properties satisfies the interface.

steps.heading: Optional and readonly properties
steps.text: Mark a property with `?` to make it optional — TypeScript will not complain if it is absent, but will require a null check before you use it. Mark it with `readonly` to prevent reassignment after the object is created, communicating that the property is immutable and catching accidental mutations at compile time. Optional and readonly can be combined on the same property.

steps.heading: Extending interfaces
steps.text: Use `extends` to build a new interface on top of an existing one — the child interface inherits all parent properties and adds its own. You can extend multiple interfaces at once: `interface C extends A, B`. This models type hierarchies cleanly: a `Post` interface might extend a base `Entity` interface that provides `id` and `createdAt`, keeping shared fields in one place.

steps.heading: Implementing an interface
steps.text: A class can `implement` an interface, making a compile-time promise to provide all required properties and methods with compatible signatures. Unlike `extends`, `implements` gives the class nothing from the interface — it must write its own implementation. This decouples the contract from the implementation, making it easy to swap one class for another as long as both implement the same interface.

steps.heading: Index signatures for dynamic keys
steps.text: When you don't know all property names up front, use an index signature (`[key: string]: T`) to allow any string key with a consistent value type. This models dictionaries, lookup tables, and config maps. All named properties on the same interface must also match that value type. `Record<string, T>` is the cleaner shorthand for the same pattern.

syntax.label: Interface declaration
syntax.note: Defines the required shape; any extra property is an error

syntax.label: Optional property (?)
syntax.note: Missing optional props are fine; present ones are type-checked

syntax.label: implements
syntax.note: Class must provide every interface member

syntax.label: Index signature
syntax.note: Allows any string key with a typed value

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use interfaces to define contracts for objects and classes, especially for public API shapes and function parameters. Prefer interfaces over type aliases for object shapes because they support extends and produce clearer error messages.
commonMistakes: Adding extra properties to an object literal assigned to an interface — TypeScript performs excess property checks on literals; extract to a variable first if you need extra fields
Confusing readonly with Object.freeze() — readonly is compile-time only; the object is still mutable at runtime unless you also freeze it
Forgetting that implements does not inherit implementation — a class implementing an interface must still provide its own method bodies

ts-generics.title: Generics
ts-generics.description: Write reusable, type-safe functions and interfaces that work with any type

steps.heading: The problem: rigid types
steps.text: Without generics you must choose between writing a separate function for every type (`firstNumber`, `firstString`, `firstBoolean`) or using `any` which discards all type information. Both options are poor at scale: the first violates DRY with identical logic repeated, the second removes the safety that makes TypeScript valuable. Generics solve this by making the type itself a parameter.

steps.heading: Generic functions with <T>
steps.text: A type parameter `<T>` is a compile-time placeholder that gets substituted with a concrete type each time the function is called. TypeScript infers `T` from the argument — call `first([1, 2, 3])` and TypeScript concludes `T = number`, making the return type `number` automatically. One generic function provides type-safe behaviour for every type, with zero duplication.

steps.heading: TypeScript infers T automatically
steps.text: TypeScript infers `T` automatically from the argument you pass — you rarely need to write the type argument explicitly. Explicit type arguments like `first<number>([1,2,3])` are only needed when inference is ambiguous, such as `useState<User | null>(null)` where `null` alone does not reveal the intended type. In all other cases, let the compiler infer and keep the call site clean.

steps.heading: Generic interfaces
steps.text: Interfaces can also have type parameters, making the shape itself reusable across different data types. `interface ApiResponse<T> { data: T; status: number }` can be used as `ApiResponse<User>`, `ApiResponse<Post[]>`, or any other type — the same response wrapper shape with different payload types. Generic interfaces are the foundation of the TypeScript standard library: `Array<T>`, `Promise<T>`, `Map<K,V>`.

steps.heading: Constraints with extends
steps.text: Use `extends` to constrain what types `T` can be — without a constraint, TypeScript assumes `T` is anything, so you cannot safely access any property on it. `<T extends { length: number }>` guarantees `T` has a `.length`, making `val.length` type-safe. The pattern `<K extends keyof T>` enables typed property lookup: `getProperty<T, K extends keyof T>(obj: T, key: K): T[K]`.

syntax.label: Generic function <T>
syntax.note: T is a placeholder replaced at call time

syntax.label: Inference at call site
syntax.note: TypeScript infers T; explicit annotation is rarely needed

syntax.label: Generic interface
syntax.note: Parameterize the shape; caller specifies T

syntax.label: Constraint with extends
syntax.note: Restricts T to types that have a length property

syntax.label: Utility types
syntax.note: Built-in mapped types; no import needed

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use generics when you find yourself writing the same function or interface multiple times for different types. They are the primary tool for reusable, type-safe utilities — array helpers, API wrappers, data containers, and custom hooks.
commonMistakes: Using any instead of a generic — any throws away type information; a generic preserves and propagates it through the call site
Over-constraining with extends — only add constraints when you need to access a specific property; unnecessary constraints reduce reusability
Confusing Partial<T> with optional properties — Partial makes all properties optional at once; use it for update/patch payloads, not as a replacement for designing interfaces correctly

ts-narrowing.title: Type Narrowing
ts-narrowing.description: Refine broad types to specific ones using type guards and control flow analysis

steps.heading: typeof and instanceof guards
steps.text: TypeScript narrows the inferred type inside conditional blocks based on the checks you perform. After `typeof val === "string"`, TypeScript knows `val` is `string` in the true branch — no type cast needed. This narrowing is automatic: TypeScript tracks which types are possible after each check and refines the type accordingly throughout the block.

steps.heading: Discriminated unions
steps.text: A discriminated union is a union of object types that each have a shared literal-typed property (the "discriminant") with a unique value per variant. Checking that property narrows the union to the specific variant in each branch automatically. This pattern is ideal for state machines, Redux actions, and any situation with a fixed set of mutually exclusive variants — a `switch` over the discriminant handles them all exhaustively.

steps.heading: Custom type guards
steps.text: A type predicate function has a return type of the form `param is Type` — when the function returns `true`, TypeScript narrows `param` to `Type` at every call site. This encapsulates reusable runtime type checks with proper TypeScript narrowing semantics. TypeScript trusts the predicate without verifying its body, so the function must actually perform a correct check.

steps.heading: Equality narrowing
steps.text: TypeScript narrows the type when you compare with `===` or `!==`. If one side is a literal value, TypeScript eliminates all union members that cannot equal it, leaving only the compatible types in the true branch. This composes naturally with discriminated unions — `if (x.kind === "circle")` narrows the entire object type — and with null checks: `if (x !== null)` makes `x` non-nullable inside the block.

steps.heading: The "in" operator
steps.text: The in operator checks whether a property exists on an object. TypeScript narrows the type based on which branch is entered — useful for discriminating between types that share some but not all properties.

steps.heading: Custom type predicates
steps.text: A user-defined type guard is a function whose return type is a type predicate (param is Type). When it returns true, TypeScript narrows the argument to that type everywhere the guard is used.

syntax.label: typeof guard
syntax.note: Works for: string, number, boolean, bigint, symbol, undefined, object, function

syntax.label: in operator
syntax.note: Checks property existence — narrows union members

syntax.label: Type predicate
syntax.note: Return type "v is T" narrows at call site

syntax.label: Discriminated union
syntax.note: Shared literal property narrows the union

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use typeof for primitives, instanceof for class instances, the in operator to distinguish object shapes, and custom predicates when you need to reuse a check or when TypeScript cannot infer it automatically.
commonMistakes: Forgetting that typeof null === "object" — always check for null explicitly before checking typeof for objects
Using as (type assertion) instead of a proper type guard — assertions bypass the type system; guards prove the type at runtime
Writing a predicate that always returns true — the predicate must actually test the runtime value, not just assert

ts-utility-types.title: Utility Types
ts-utility-types.description: Built-in TypeScript types that transform and derive new types from existing ones

steps.heading: Partial and Required
steps.text: `Partial<T>` transforms every property in `T` to optional, creating a "draft" version of the type — ideal for `PATCH` request bodies or partially-filled form state. `Required<T>` is the inverse: it removes `?` from every property, giving you a fully-populated version. Both derive a new type from an existing interface without rewriting the property list, keeping your types in sync automatically.

steps.heading: Pick and Omit
steps.text: `Pick<T, K>` creates a new type containing only the property names you list — ideal for focused view models or component props. `Omit<T, K>` is the complement: it copies all properties except the listed keys. Use `Omit<User, "password">` for safe public-facing types, or `Omit<User, "id" | "createdAt">` for creation payloads where those fields are generated by the database.

steps.heading: Record, Readonly, and ReturnType
steps.text: `Record<K, V>` creates an object type where every key is `K` and every value is `V` — the canonical way to type dictionaries. `Readonly<T>` marks all properties as immutable at the type level. `ReturnType<F>` extracts a function's return type so you don't duplicate it. Combine them: `Readonly<Partial<User>>` builds a safe update payload in one expression.

steps.heading: Exclude, Extract, and NonNullable
steps.text: Exclude<T, U> removes from T all types that are assignable to U. Extract<T, U> keeps only types assignable to U. NonNullable<T> removes null and undefined — useful for values that have been validated.

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use utility types instead of duplicating interface definitions. Partial and Omit are essential for API update payloads. Pick creates focused view-models. Record builds dictionaries.
commonMistakes: Defining a separate "optional" interface by hand — use Partial<T> or Pick<T, K> instead; they stay in sync when the base type changes
Confusing Exclude and Omit — Omit removes keys from an object type; Exclude filters members from a union type
Not using NonNullable after validation — if you have checked that a value is not null, NonNullable communicates that to TypeScript

ts-arrays.title: Arrays & Tuples
ts-arrays.description: Typed arrays with push enforcement and fixed-length tuples for positional data

steps.heading: Typed arrays
steps.text: TypeScript allows you to specify that an array should only contain elements of a particular type. Use the `T[]` syntax or the generic `Array<T>` form — both are equivalent. TypeScript uses this information to type the return values of array methods: `scores.map(s => s * 2)` returns `number[]` automatically. The compiler also catches type violations like pushing a string into a `number[]` array before the code runs.

steps.heading: Arrays of objects
steps.text: You can type arrays of custom object shapes. TypeScript checks each element against the type, and gives you autocomplete for all properties when you iterate. Iteration methods like `map`, `filter`, and `find` preserve the element type through the chain — `users.filter(u => u.active)` returns `User[]` and `users.find(u => u.id === id)` returns `User | undefined`. You get full type safety at every step without writing extra annotations.

steps.heading: Tuples — fixed-length arrays
steps.text: A tuple is a typed array with a fixed number of elements where each position has its own type. Tuples are useful for positional data like coordinates, key-value pairs, or function return values.

steps.heading: Labeled tuples
steps.text: TypeScript supports labeled tuple elements for better readability. Labels do not change the type — they only appear in editor tooltips — but they make the intent much clearer than unnamed positional slots.

syntax.label: Typed array (T[])
syntax.note: Most common syntax; T[] and Array<T> are equivalent

syntax.label: Generic array (Array<T>)
syntax.note: More readable for complex nested types

syntax.label: Tuple
