syntax.note: Fixed-length; each index has its own type

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use T[] for any homogeneous collection. Use tuples when you have a fixed set of positional values with different types — especially for function return values that bundle related data without needing a full object.
commonMistakes: Using any[] — this defeats type checking on array contents; use the specific element type instead
Exceeding tuple length — TypeScript allows push on non-readonly tuples, which can corrupt positional assumptions; use readonly tuples when immutability matters
Confusing tuples with union arrays — [string, number] is a two-element tuple; (string | number)[] is an array of any length with mixed types

ts-classes.title: Classes & Inheritance
ts-classes.description: TypeScript classes with typed properties, access modifiers, and abstract base classes

steps.heading: Class syntax with types
steps.text: TypeScript classes work like JavaScript classes but with type annotations. Declare property types at the top of the class body before the constructor. TypeScript enforces that the constructor initializes every non-optional property.

steps.heading: readonly and optional properties
steps.text: Mark a property `readonly` to prevent mutation after construction — it can only be assigned inside the constructor. Mark it optional with `?` when it may not always be provided; TypeScript then requires an `undefined` check before accessing it. You can combine both modifiers: `readonly email?: string` is a property that is set once and may be absent.

steps.heading: Access modifiers
steps.text: TypeScript adds three access modifiers: public (default — accessible everywhere), private (only within the class), and protected (within the class and subclasses). The # prefix is JavaScript's native private field — enforced at runtime, not just compile time.

steps.heading: Inheritance with extends
steps.text: A class can extend another class to inherit its public and protected members. The subclass must call super() before accessing this. Override methods to provide specialized behavior.

steps.heading: Abstract classes
steps.text: An abstract class cannot be instantiated directly — it exists to be extended. Abstract methods have no implementation in the base class; every concrete subclass must implement them.

syntax.label: readonly
syntax.note: Can be set in constructor only

syntax.label: implements
syntax.note: Class must provide all interface members

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use classes when you need encapsulation, inheritance, or shared behavior across instances. Prefer abstract classes over interfaces when you also want to share concrete method implementations. For simple data containers, prefer interfaces or type aliases.
commonMistakes: Forgetting super() in subclass constructors — TypeScript errors if you access this before calling super()
Using private when you need protected — private members are inaccessible in subclasses; use protected when subclasses need them
Overusing class-based OOP in React — React components are best as functions; only reach for classes when modeling domain entities with behavior

ts-enums.title: Enums
ts-enums.description: Named constants with numeric or string values — and exhaustive switch checks

steps.heading: Numeric enums
steps.text: An enum declares a set of named constants. By default, values are auto-incremented numbers starting at 0. You can override the start value or set explicit values for any member.

steps.heading: String enums
steps.text: String enums require explicit string values for every member. They are more readable in logs and debugging because the value itself is meaningful — unlike numeric enums where you only see a number.

steps.heading: Exhaustive switch with never
steps.text: When every enum value is handled in a switch, TypeScript knows the default branch is unreachable. Assigning to never in the default catches missing cases at compile time.

steps.heading: Real-world use: RBAC roles
steps.text: String enums are well-suited for roles, statuses, and configuration constants that appear in logs, APIs, and databases — the string value is self-documenting rather than an opaque number. TypeScript catches any attempt to pass a plain string literal where the enum type is expected, preventing typos at compile time.

syntax.label: String enum
syntax.note: Values are readable strings — preferred for APIs/logs

syntax.label: Const enum
syntax.note: Faster but cannot be iterated at runtime

syntax.label: Enum as type
syntax.note: Only enum members pass the type check

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use string enums for status values, roles, and configuration keys that appear in APIs, logs, and databases — the string value is self-documenting. Use const enum for compile-time constants that are never iterated. Prefer string unions ("draft" | "published") over enums in React props — they are simpler and require no import.
commonMistakes: Using numeric enums where the value appears in logs or APIs — the number has no meaning; use string enums instead
Forgetting that TypeScript numeric enums are two-way — Direction[0] gives "Up", which can cause unexpected behavior
Using enums in React component props — prefer string literal unions which are lighter and require no import

ts-type-assertion.title: Type Assertion
ts-type-assertion.description: Override TypeScript's inferred type with as, safely access nullable values with ?., and provide fallbacks with ??

steps.heading: The as keyword
steps.text: Type assertion tells TypeScript "trust me, I know the type better than you do." Use the as keyword (not angle brackets — they conflict with JSX). The assertion only affects the compiler, never the runtime value.

steps.heading: JSON and API responses
steps.text: When you parse JSON or receive data from an API, TypeScript infers the type as any or unknown. Asserting a specific type immediately after parsing gives you full type safety downstream — but you are responsible for the correctness of the assertion.

steps.heading: Limitations and non-null assertion
steps.text: Type assertion cannot convert completely incompatible types. The non-null assertion operator ! asserts that a value is neither null nor undefined. Use it sparingly: if the value really can be null at runtime, the assertion will not protect you.

syntax.label: as assertion
syntax.note: Prefer as over angle brackets — compatible with JSX

syntax.label: Non-null assertion (!)
syntax.note: Use only when you are certain the value exists

syntax.label: Double assertion
syntax.note: Last resort — signals something is wrong with the types

syntax.label: as const
syntax.note: Narrows to literal types and makes object readonly

syntax.label: Optional chaining (?.)
syntax.note: Returns undefined instead of throwing on null/undefined — works for properties, indexes, and calls

syntax.label: Nullish coalescing (??)
syntax.note: Prefer over || when 0, empty string, and false are valid values

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use type assertion when TypeScript cannot infer the correct type from context — typically when selecting DOM elements, parsing JSON, or working with weakly-typed third-party libraries. Always prefer a runtime type guard over a bare assertion when the value comes from an external source.
commonMistakes: Using as to silence type errors instead of fixing the underlying issue — assertions bypass the type system; fix the root cause instead
Overusing non-null assertion (!) — each ! is a promise to TypeScript that can crash at runtime if the value is actually null
Double assertions (as unknown as T) — always a design smell; use proper generics or type guards instead
Using || instead of ?? for default values when 0 or empty string are valid — ?? only falls back on null/undefined, || falls back on all falsy values

ts-react.title: TypeScript in React
ts-react.description: Type props, state, and hooks in React components for safer, self-documenting code

steps.heading: Typing Props
steps.text: Define a type alias for your component's props. TypeScript will error if required props are missing or have the wrong type. Mark optional props with ?. Use React.ReactNode for children. Union types constrain allowed values — great for status/variant props.

steps.heading: Typing State
steps.text: useState accepts a generic: useState<T>(initial). When initial state is null or undefined, TypeScript cannot infer the full type — always annotate explicitly. For complex objects, define a type alias and pass it as the generic. TypeScript then prevents you from setting state with the wrong shape.

steps.heading: Typing useRef & useReducer
steps.text: useRef needs an explicit element type — TypeScript cannot guess whether it points to an input, a div, or a canvas. Pass the HTML element type as a generic and null as the initial value. useReducer benefits most from TypeScript: define State and a discriminated union for Action, and the exhaustive default branch catches missing cases at compile time.

steps.heading: useCallback & useMemo
steps.text: useCallback caches a function so its reference stays stable across renders — pass the typed function directly. useMemo caches a computed value and only recomputes when dependencies change. TypeScript infers the return type of useMemo from the callback. Both are most valuable when passed to child components via props or used as useEffect dependencies.

steps.heading: Typing API responses
steps.text: Define a type for your API response and pass it as the generic to useState and fetch wrappers. A generic fetch utility fetchData<T>() fetches a URL and asserts the JSON matches T — giving you full type safety downstream. Use Partial<T> for PATCH operations where only some fields are sent.

steps.heading: Typing DOM Events
steps.text: React provides specific event handler types for each HTML element. Use MouseEventHandler<Element>, ChangeEventHandler<Element>, and FormEventHandler<Element> so TypeScript knows which properties are available on the event object.

syntax.label: Props type
syntax.note: Use type alias (not interface) for props — no accidental merging

syntax.label: useState with generic
syntax.note: Required when initial value is null/undefined

syntax.label: useRef
syntax.note: Always type the HTML element; use optional chaining

syntax.label: useReducer action union
syntax.note: Discriminated union — exhaustive switch catches missing cases

syntax.label: children prop
syntax.note: ReactNode covers all valid JSX child types

syntax.label: Generic fetch
syntax.note: Assert the response type — T flows to callers

syntax.label: Event handler types
syntax.note: Specific handler types give accurate event.target

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Always define a type alias for component props — even for simple components. Annotate useState when the initial value is null, undefined, or an empty array (TypeScript cannot infer the full shape). Use generics for reusable fetch utilities and hooks.
commonMistakes: Using interface for props — interface declarations can accidentally merge in the same scope; type aliases cannot
Letting useState infer from null — useState(null) gives type null, not string | null; always pass the generic
Skipping types on useRef — untyped refs produce any; always pass the HTML element type
Using any to silence errors — any disables all checking downstream; prefer unknown and narrow the type

ts-zod.title: Zod — Runtime Validation
ts-zod.description: Validate external data at runtime and infer TypeScript types from schemas — one source of truth

steps.heading: TypeScript's blind spot
steps.text: TypeScript types only exist during development. When compiled to JavaScript, every interface and type is erased. Data arriving at runtime — from APIs, forms, or localStorage — is never checked by TypeScript. You can assert a type with `as`, but that's a promise to the compiler, not a real check. If the API changes shape, your app crashes silently.

steps.heading: Define schemas with Zod
steps.text: Zod is a TypeScript-first schema validation library. You define a schema using Zod primitives — z.string(), z.number(), z.boolean(), z.object(). The schema is a real JavaScript object that exists at runtime and can validate incoming data.

steps.heading: safeParse — validate without throwing
steps.text: Call safeParse() with the raw data. It returns { success, data, error } — never throws. Check success before accessing data. If validation fails, error contains detailed information about every field that did not match.

steps.heading: z.infer — one source of truth
steps.text: Instead of writing a TypeScript type AND a Zod schema for the same thing, derive the type directly from the schema using z.infer. The schema becomes the single source of truth: change the schema, and the type updates automatically.

syntax.label: safeParse
syntax.note: Never throws — always check success first

syntax.label: parse
syntax.note: Use when you want to throw on invalid input

syntax.label: z.infer
syntax.note: Derive TypeScript type from schema — single source of truth

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use Zod whenever data crosses a boundary you do not control: API responses, form submissions, URL parameters, localStorage values. Define the schema once and derive the TypeScript type from it with z.infer — never maintain a separate type and validator for the same shape.
commonMistakes: Using as User instead of parsing with Zod — type assertions are promises to the compiler, not runtime checks; the assertion silently passes even if the data has the wrong shape
Calling parse() instead of safeParse() in user-facing code — parse() throws a ZodError that can crash a component; safeParse() returns success/error so you can handle it gracefully
Forgetting to derive the type with z.infer — if you write a separate TypeScript type, it can drift out of sync with the schema; always use z.infer as the single source of truth

testing-basics.title: Testing Basics & Vitest
testing-basics.description: Why tests matter, the testing pyramid, and writing your first unit tests with Vitest

steps.heading: Why test?
steps.text: Tests are automated checks that verify your code behaves correctly. Without them, every change risks breaking something silently. A good test suite gives you confidence to refactor, catches regressions before they reach production, and acts as executable documentation showing how the code is meant to be used.

steps.heading: The Testing Pyramid
steps.text: Group tests by scope and cost. Unit tests (base) isolate a single function — fast, cheap, write many. Integration tests exercise collaboration between modules (e.g. React + API). End-to-End tests (top) click through a real browser — slow and brittle, keep few. The wider base means: write many cheap unit tests, fewer integration tests, very few E2E tests.

steps.heading: Vitest setup
steps.text: Vitest is a Vite-native test runner — it reuses your vite.config.ts so aliases and plugins work in tests too. Install it as a dev dependency, add a test script, and Vitest will auto-discover any *.test.ts or *.spec.ts files. Add vitest run to your build script so tests block the production build on failure.

steps.heading: Test anatomy
steps.text: describe() groups related tests. test() (alias: it()) defines a single case. expect() creates an assertion — it compares the actual result against the expected value. A test passes when all assertions pass and fails when any throws. Name tests with plain English: what does it do, under what condition.

steps.heading: Common matchers
steps.text: Vitest ships with Jest-compatible matchers. toBe uses Object.is (strict) — use it for primitives. toEqual does deep equality — use it for objects and arrays. toContain checks array membership. resolves/rejects handle async assertions. .not inverts any matcher.

syntax.label: describe + test
syntax.note: it() is an alias for test()

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Write unit tests for every pure function and utility. Run tests in watch mode during development. Add vitest run to your build script so broken tests block deployment.
commonMistakes: Using toBe for objects — toBe uses Object.is (reference equality); use toEqual for deep comparison
Forgetting await on async assertions — without await, the assertion resolves after the test passes
Writing tests after the fact only — writing tests first (TDD) forces you to think about the API before implementation
Testing implementation details — test behaviour (inputs → outputs), not internals (private variables, hooks logic)

testing-react.title: React Testing Library
testing-react.description: Test React components the way users interact with them — by role, text, and label, not implementation

steps.heading: React Testing Library philosophy
steps.text: RTL's guiding principle: "The more your tests resemble how your software is used, the more confidence they give you." Query the DOM the way a real user would — by accessible role, label text, or visible content — not by class names or component internals. This makes tests resilient to refactors that leave behaviour unchanged.

steps.heading: Queries: finding elements
steps.text: RTL provides three query families. getBy* throws if not found (use for elements that must exist). queryBy* returns null (use to assert absence). findBy* is async — awaits element appearance. The preferred query is ByRole — it uses ARIA roles and mirrors how screen readers navigate. ByLabelText and ByText are next in priority.

steps.heading: Firing events & user interactions
steps.text: Use fireEvent for simple, synchronous DOM events. For more realistic user interactions (typing, clicking, tabbing), prefer @testing-library/user-event — it fires the same sequence of events a real browser would. Always await userEvent actions. Test what users can do, not how the DOM is structured.

steps.heading: Mocks & spies
steps.text: vi.fn() creates a spy — a function that records calls. Use it to replace dependencies (fetch, callbacks, modules) with controlled stand-ins. vi.stubGlobal() replaces globals like fetch. beforeEach sets up the mock fresh for each test; afterEach with vi.restoreAllMocks() cleans up. Check calls with toHaveBeenCalledWith.

steps.heading: Integration testing
steps.text: Integration tests render a component together with its real dependencies (hooks, context, router) and test end-to-end flows. Wrap the component in any providers it needs. Use waitFor() to wait for async state updates. Test the full user journey — form fill → submit → success message — not isolated pieces.

syntax.label: render + screen
syntax.note: screen searches the full rendered DOM

syntax.label: Query priority
syntax.note: getBy throws; queryBy returns null; findBy is async

syntax.label: Mocking fetch
syntax.note: Always restore mocks after each test

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Test every user-facing behaviour: what appears after a click, what shows during loading, what renders on error. Do not test implementation details like state variables or internal component methods.
commonMistakes: Querying by class name or test-id — prefer ByRole and ByLabelText; they test what users actually see
Not awaiting findBy or waitFor — async state updates happen after the assertion without await, causing false passes
Forgetting to restore mocks — leftover stubs bleed into later tests and cause flaky failures
Rendering without providers — if a component uses Context or Router, wrap it in the same providers as your real app

testing-ci.title: Coverage & CI with GitHub Actions
testing-ci.description: Read coverage reports, set thresholds, and automate testing on every push with a GitHub Actions pipeline

steps.heading: Coverage reports
steps.text: Coverage measures which lines, branches, and functions your tests actually execute. Vitest uses v8 or istanbul to collect it. Run with --coverage to see a report. A high number does not guarantee correctness — you still need meaningful assertions — but it highlights untested paths that may hide bugs.

steps.heading: Reading a coverage report
steps.text: The text report shows four metrics per file. Stmts: percentage of statements executed. Branch: percentage of if/else paths taken (most important — reveals dead conditional branches). Funcs: percentage of functions called. Lines: percentage of source lines hit. Uncovered lines are shown in the rightmost column — start there.

steps.heading: CI/CD with GitHub Actions
steps.text: Continuous Integration runs your test suite automatically on every push or pull request. GitHub Actions reads a YAML workflow file from .github/workflows/. Define the trigger (on:), the environment (runs-on:), and the steps (install → test → build → deploy). A failed test step aborts the rest of the pipeline.

steps.heading: Deploy to GitHub Pages
steps.text: Add a deploy step to publish the Vite production build to GitHub Pages automatically after tests pass. Set base in vite.config.ts to your repo name. The actions/deploy-pages action handles uploading the dist/ folder. After merging to main, your site is live within minutes — zero manual steps.

syntax.label: Coverage thresholds
syntax.note: Pipeline fails if coverage drops below threshold

syntax.label: GitHub Actions trigger
syntax.note: Runs on every push and PR to main

syntax.label: Workflow steps
syntax.note: Any failed step aborts the rest

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Set up CI from day one on any project. Aim for 70–80% coverage on business logic. 100% coverage is not the goal — meaningful tests on critical paths are.
commonMistakes: Chasing 100% coverage — coverage measures execution, not correctness; 100% with bad assertions is meaningless
Using npm install in CI instead of npm ci — npm ci is faster and guarantees reproducible installs from the lockfile
Not caching node_modules — add cache: npm to setup-node to avoid re-downloading dependencies on every run
Running tests without --coverage flag — without it, no report is generated and thresholds are not checked

git-intro.title: What is Git?
git-intro.description: Distributed version control — why it exists, how it works, and the 4 key areas every developer uses daily

steps.heading: The problem: who has the latest version?
steps.text: When multiple developers work on the same project, files get emailed, overwritten, or lost. Version control solves this by giving every change a history — so you always know what changed, when, and why.

steps.heading: Working Directory
steps.text: Your local files on disk. This is where you write and edit code. Git watches this folder but does not automatically save anything — you decide what gets tracked. Every commit is a snapshot of the entire project, identified by a SHA-1 hash that makes the history immutable and tamper-evident.

steps.heading: Staging Area
steps.text: A holding area for changes you want to include in the next snapshot. Use git add to move files here. You can stage some files and leave others out — giving you precise control over what you commit.

steps.heading: Local Repository
steps.text: The .git folder on your machine stores the full project history. Every commit is a permanent snapshot. You can always travel back to any previous state — nothing is ever truly lost.

steps.heading: All 4 areas together
steps.text: The full Git flow: Working Directory → Staging Area → Local Repo → Remote Repo. Changes move forward through these areas and can flow back (`git checkout`, `git pull`) when needed.

syntax.label: Check current status
syntax.note: shows modified, staged, and untracked files

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use git init for new projects, git clone to work on existing ones. Always git status before committing to see what's changed.
commonMistakes: Committing without a message — always write a short, meaningful message describing what changed and why
Running git add . blindly — check git status first so you don't accidentally stage build files or secrets
Thinking git saves automatically — nothing is saved until you explicitly git add and git commit

git-workflow.title: Git Workflow
git-workflow.description: The daily development loop — branching, staging, committing, and pushing changes

steps.heading: The daily Git loop
steps.text: Every day in a development team follows the same pattern: get the latest code, create a branch, make changes, stage them, commit, then push to share with the team. The `main` branch always stays deployable — feature branches keep work in progress isolated until it is ready for review.

steps.heading: Start: init or clone
steps.text: For a brand new project use `git init`. To work on an existing project, `git clone` downloads the full history and sets up the remote tracking automatically. You only do this once per project — from then on, `git pull` stays in sync. `HEAD` is the pointer tracking which branch you are currently on.

steps.heading: Create a branch
steps.text: Always work in a branch — never directly on `main`. A branch is an isolated copy of the codebase where your changes stay contained until you decide to merge them back. Branches are lightweight in Git — creating one just adds a pointer and costs nothing. Delete them freely after merging.

steps.heading: Stage and commit
steps.text: Make changes, stage the relevant files, then commit with a descriptive message. Commit often — small commits are easier to review, understand, and revert if something goes wrong.

steps.heading: Push to remote
steps.text: Push your branch to the remote repository so teammates can see it and you can open a pull request. The `-u` flag sets the upstream tracking so future pushes just need `git push`. After the PR is merged, pull the updated `main` and delete the merged branch locally.

syntax.label: Create + switch to branch
syntax.note: shorthand for git branch + git checkout

syntax.label: Stage all changes
syntax.note: stages all modified and new files

syntax.label: Pull latest from remote
syntax.note: fetch + merge remote changes into current branch

commonMistakes: Working directly on main — always create a feature branch so your changes don't disrupt the stable codebase
Forgetting to pull before starting new work — stale code causes merge conflicts; always git pull first
Giant commits — commit small logical units of work; it's easier to review and safer to revert

git-gitignore.title: .gitignore
git-gitignore.description: Tell Git which files to never track — secrets, dependencies, and build output stay out of your repo

steps.heading: Without .gitignore — everything gets committed
steps.text: Run git add . without a .gitignore and you'll accidentally commit node_modules (200MB+), your .env secrets, and build output. Once in git history, they're hard to remove completely.

steps.heading: Create a .gitignore file
steps.text: Add a file named .gitignore in the root of your project. List what to ignore — Git will never stage or commit those files. Commit the .gitignore itself so teammates get the same rules.

steps.heading: Pattern syntax
steps.text: Each line is a pattern. Use `/` to match directories, `*` as a wildcard, `**` to cross directory boundaries, and `!` to un-ignore a specific file. Patterns are matched from the project root. GitHub provides a collection of standard templates for every language and framework.

steps.heading: With .gitignore — only your code is tracked
steps.text: After adding `.gitignore`, `git add .` only stages your actual source files. Secrets stay local, `node_modules` stays off GitHub, and your repo stays lean. If you accidentally committed a file before ignoring it, use `git rm --cached` to stop tracking it — and rotate any secrets that were exposed.

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Create a .gitignore as the very first step in every new project, before your first commit. Use gitignore.io or GitHub's templates for your tech stack.
commonMistakes: Committing node_modules or .env before adding .gitignore — to fix, use git rm --cached and then add the rules
Forgetting the trailing slash on directories — "node_modules" ignores a file called node_modules; "node_modules/" ignores the folder
Adding .gitignore rules after the files are already tracked — git ignores only untracked files; already-committed files need git rm --cached first

git-github.title: GitHub
git-github.description: GitHub is not Git — it's a platform that hosts repos online and adds collaboration features like Pull Requests

steps.heading: Git ≠ GitHub
steps.text: Git is a command-line tool that runs on your machine. GitHub is a website that hosts Git repositories online. You can use Git without GitHub, but GitHub needs Git. Think of Git as the engine and GitHub as the garage.

steps.heading: Remote repository
steps.text: GitHub stores a copy of your repository in the cloud. Every team member clones it once, then uses git pull to get the latest changes and git push to share their work. The remote is the single source of truth.

steps.heading: Pull Requests
steps.text: A Pull Request (PR) is a GitHub feature that lets you propose merging a branch into main. Before merging, teammates can review the code, leave comments, request changes, and approve. This keeps the main branch clean and reviewed.

steps.heading: Forking & open source
steps.text: Fork creates your own copy of someone else's repository. You can make changes freely, then open a PR back to the original. This is how millions of developers contribute to open source projects without needing direct access.

syntax.label: Add remote
syntax.note: "origin" is the conventional name for the main remote

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use GitHub (or GitLab/Bitbucket) whenever collaborating with others or backing up your work. Always use Pull Requests for code that others depend on — never push directly to main on shared projects.
commonMistakes: Pushing directly to main on a shared repo — always use PRs so changes can be reviewed before they affect everyone
Confusing git pull with git fetch — git pull merges immediately; git fetch just downloads without merging
Forgetting to add a remote — a local git repo has no connection to GitHub until you git remote add origin <url>

git-collab-setup.title: Team Collaboration Setup
git-collab-setup.description: How to set up a shared GitHub repo, invite collaborators, protect main, and run the team PR workflow

steps.heading: Create a repo and invite your team
steps.text: Start on GitHub: create a new repository, then go to Settings → Collaborators → Add people. Teammates receive an email invite — they must accept before they can push. For organisations, use Teams to manage access at scale.

steps.heading: Everyone clones the same remote
steps.text: Every team member runs git clone once. This downloads the full history and sets origin as the remote name automatically. From then on they use git pull to sync and git push to share.

steps.heading: Protect main — require Pull Request reviews
steps.text: Go to Settings → Branches → Add branch protection rule for main. Enable "Require a pull request before merging" and "Require approvals". This prevents anyone from pushing directly to main — all changes must go through a reviewed PR.

steps.heading: The daily team loop
steps.text: Every feature or fix follows the same cycle: pull latest main, create a branch, commit your work, push the branch, open a PR, get a review, then merge. Small focused PRs are easier to review and less likely to conflict.

syntax.label: Sync with remote main
syntax.note: do this before starting new work

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use branch protection on every shared repo from day one. Every team member works in branches — never directly in main. Use PRs for all changes, even small ones, so changes are visible and reviewed.
commonMistakes: Pushing directly to main — without branch protection anyone can accidentally break the shared codebase
Long-lived branches — the longer a branch lives, the more conflicts it collects; merge small and often
Forgetting to pull before branching — always git pull origin main first, otherwise your branch starts from stale code

git-merge-conflicts.title: Merge Conflicts
git-merge-conflicts.description: What happens when two branches edit the same line — and how to resolve it step by step

steps.heading: Conflicts happen when two branches edit the same line
steps.text: When you merge a branch and both sides modified the same part of a file, Git can't decide automatically which version to keep. It pauses the merge and asks you to resolve it manually.

steps.heading: Git inserts conflict markers into the file
steps.text: Git marks the conflict directly in the file with <<<<<<< (your branch), ======= (separator), and >>>>>>> (the incoming branch). Everything between the markers is the content in conflict.

steps.heading: Edit the file to resolve
steps.text: Open the file in your editor. Decide what to keep — your version, their version, or a combination. Delete all three marker lines completely. Most editors (VS Code) show a visual conflict resolver with one-click options.

steps.heading: Mark resolved and finish the merge
steps.text: After editing all conflicted files, stage them with `git add` to mark them resolved, then run `git commit`. Git writes a default merge commit message like "Merge branch 'feature' into main". Before committing, confirm no conflict markers (`<<<<<<<`) remain — staging a file that still contains them silently puts broken code in your repo.

syntax.label: Finish merge
syntax.note: Git provides the merge commit message

syntax.label: Abort the merge
syntax.note: cancel and go back to before merge

commonMistakes: Leaving marker lines (<<<, ===, >>>) in the file — always remove ALL markers; the code won't work otherwise
Running git commit before staging all resolved files — use git status to confirm no "both modified" files remain
Panicking and git merge --abort unnecessarily — conflicts look scary but are just a text edit; take it step by step

git-undo-stash.title: Undo & Stash
git-undo-stash.description: Three levels of undo in Git — discard changes, uncommit, or safely reverse on shared branches

steps.heading: 3 tools for undoing in Git
steps.text: Git gives you different undo tools depending on where in the workflow you are. git restore discards uncommitted edits, git reset moves the HEAD pointer back, and git revert is the safe option for shared branches.

steps.heading: git restore — discard working directory changes
steps.text: Throws away all edits to a file and restores it to its last committed state. Use `--staged` to unstage without discarding changes. Warning: this cannot be undone — uncommitted changes that have never been committed cannot be recovered. Use `git stash` instead if you want to save the changes temporarily.

steps.heading: git stash — shelve work temporarily
steps.text: `git stash` saves your uncommitted changes on a stack and gives you a clean working directory. Perfect when you need to switch branches mid-work without losing anything. `git stash pop` re-applies the most recent stash and removes it from the stack. The stash persists across Git operations, so you can stash, switch branches, fix a bug, switch back, and pop to resume exactly where you left off.

steps.heading: git revert — safe undo on shared branches
steps.text: `git revert <hash>` creates a new commit that undoes a previous commit — the original commit stays in history. Because no history is rewritten, the result pushes normally without force. It's the correct tool for undoing mistakes on shared branches like `main`. Use `git reset` only for commits that have never been pushed.

syntax.label: Restore stash
syntax.note: applies last stash and removes it

syntax.label: Safe undo commit
syntax.note: creates new undo commit — safe for shared branches

syntax.label: Uncommit locally
syntax.note: moves HEAD back 1 — never use on pushed commits

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use git restore for quick discard of local edits. Use git stash when switching context. Use git revert on any branch that others have pulled from — never git reset on public history.
commonMistakes: Using git reset on already-pushed commits — this rewrites history and will force-push errors or confuse teammates; use git revert instead
git restore . with unsaved work you meant to keep — there is no undo; double-check git status before running
Losing stashes — git stash drop removes a stash permanently; use git stash list to confirm before dropping

nextjs-basics.title: Next.js
nextjs-basics.description: Build fullstack React apps with the App Router — server components, data fetching, server functions, and error handling

steps.heading: What is Next.js?
steps.text: Next.js is a React framework for production apps that provides routing, data fetching, code-splitting, and deployment out of the box. With React 19 and React Server Components (RSC), it now embraces a fullstack model: components can run permanently on the server, fetch data without useEffect, and query databases directly — all in the same component tree as your client-side UI.

steps.heading: App Router & project structure
steps.text: Create a Next.js project with `npx create-next-app@latest`. The App Router uses the file system to define routes: every folder under `src/app/` becomes a URL segment. Special files control what renders at each segment — page.js is the UI, layout.js wraps it with shared chrome, loading.js shows a Suspense fallback, and error.js catches uncaught exceptions.

steps.heading: Server vs Client Components
steps.text: In the App Router, every component is a Server Component by default — it runs on the server, can be async, and never ships to the client bundle. Add 'use client' at the top of a file to opt into a Client Component when you need interactivity, state, or browser APIs. The rule: start with Server Components, reach for Client Components only when necessary.

steps.heading: Data fetching & streaming
steps.text: Server Components can use fetch() or call a database client directly — no API layer needed. For slow data, pass a Promise to a Client Component and use use() with Suspense to stream content progressively. A loading.js file is the shortcut: Next.js automatically wraps the page in a Suspense boundary and shows the loading UI while rendering.

steps.heading: Server Functions & mutations
steps.text: A Server Function is an async function marked with 'use server' — it runs exclusively on the server but can be called from any component, including Client Components. Use them as form action props to handle submissions without a separate API endpoint. After mutating data, call revalidatePath() or revalidateTag() to purge the cache and trigger a re-render.

steps.heading: Error handling
steps.text: Next.js splits errors into two categories. Expected errors (validation, 404s) should be handled explicitly — return structured state from Server Functions with useActionState, check res.ok from fetch, or call notFound() for missing resources. Unexpected errors are caught by error.js boundaries — a Client Component that receives the error and a reset() function to retry.

syntax.label: Server Component
syntax.note: async, can fetch/query DB, never in browser bundle

syntax.label: Client Component
syntax.note: 'use client' must be the first line

syntax.label: Server Function
syntax.note: Called as form action or from Client Components

syntax.label: Suspense streaming
syntax.note: Client Component uses use(promise) to unwrap

syntax.label: loading.js
syntax.note: Automatically wraps page.js in a Suspense boundary

syntax.label: error.js
syntax.note: Catches uncaught exceptions in this route segment

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use Next.js when you need a production-ready fullstack React setup with built-in routing, server rendering, and deployment optimizations. Prefer plain React + Vite when you are building a client-only SPA without server-side data requirements.
commonMistakes: Using useState or useEffect in a Server Component — these are client-only APIs; add 'use client' or move the logic to a child Client Component
Fetching in a Client Component when a Server Component would do — every client fetch adds a round-trip; prefer server-side data access
Forgetting revalidatePath() after a mutation — stale cached data will show old values until Next.js is told to revalidate
Putting 'use server' at the top of a file that also exports a default component — each directive scopes its file; keep Server Functions in separate action files

nextjs-routing.title: File-based Routing
nextjs-routing.description: Next.js maps folders to URL segments — create a folder, get a route. Dynamic segments, route groups, and catch-all routes give you full control without a router config.

steps.heading: Folders = Routes
steps.text: Every folder inside `src/app/` becomes a URL path segment. A file named `page.tsx` inside that folder makes it a real route. No router config, no imports — just create the folder and file. Nested folders create nested URLs.

steps.heading: Dynamic Segments
steps.text: Wrap a folder name in square brackets to create a dynamic segment: `[id]` matches /posts/1, /posts/abc, etc. Read the value with the `params` prop — in Next.js 15+ `params` must be awaited.

steps.heading: Route Groups
steps.text: Wrap a folder name in parentheses to create a route group: `(marketing)` groups routes without adding to the URL. Use this to share layouts across routes that are not nested in the URL.

steps.heading: Catch-all Segments
steps.text: Use `[...slug]` to match multiple path segments at once. `params.slug` is an array of the segments. Add double brackets `[[...slug]]` to make the catch-all optional — it then also matches the root path.

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use file-based routing as your primary routing strategy — no configuration needed. Use route groups to share layouts without affecting URLs. Use dynamic segments for content-driven pages like blog posts, product pages, and user profiles.
commonMistakes: Forgetting that page.tsx is required — creating a folder without page.tsx creates a layout-only segment that renders no UI
Reading params without awaiting in Next.js 15+ — params is now a Promise; always await it
Naming the catch-all file incorrectly — it must be [...slug] not [slug] to match multiple segments

nextjs-layouts.title: Layouts & Special Files
nextjs-layouts.description: Nest layouts without re-rendering, add metadata for SEO, and use special files to handle loading, errors, and not-found states at every route level.

steps.heading: Nested Layouts
steps.text: `layout.tsx` wraps `page.tsx` and all child routes. Layouts persist across navigations — they do not re-render when the child page changes. Nest layout files to build complex UI: a root layout with html and body, a dashboard layout with a sidebar, a settings layout with a tab bar.

steps.heading: Special Files
steps.text: Next.js reserves specific filenames at each route segment. `loading.tsx` shows a skeleton while the page renders. `error.tsx` catches unhandled exceptions and must be a Client Component. `not-found.tsx` renders when `notFound()` is called. `template.tsx` re-renders on every navigation — useful for page transitions.

steps.heading: Metadata API
steps.text: Export a metadata object or a generateMetadata() function from any `page.tsx` or `layout.tsx` to set head tags. Static metadata is exported as a constant. Dynamic metadata uses generateMetadata() — an `async` function that receives the same `params` as the page.

steps.heading: RootLayout Requirements
steps.text: Every Next.js app must have `app/layout.tsx` — the root layout. It must include html and body tags. It wraps every page and is the right place for global styles, analytics, auth providers, and theme providers.

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use layouts for persistent UI (nav, sidebar, footer). Use loading.tsx to prevent layout shift during data fetches. Use the metadata API instead of manually adding head tags — it handles deduplication and inheritance automatically.
commonMistakes: Forgetting html + body in the root layout — Next.js will throw an error if they are missing
Using error.tsx as a Server Component — it must have 'use client' to use the reset() function
Overusing template.tsx — unlike layout, it re-mounts on every navigation which can hurt performance

nextjs-navigation.title: Navigation
nextjs-navigation.description: Link for declarative navigation with prefetching, useRouter for programmatic navigation, and redirect() / notFound() for server-side routing.

steps.heading: Link Component
steps.text: Use `Link` from `next/link` instead of a for client-side navigation — no full page reload. Next.js automatically prefetches linked pages in the background when they appear in the viewport, making navigation feel instant.

steps.heading: useRouter — Programmatic Navigation
steps.text: Use `useRouter` from `next/navigation` in Client Components when you need to navigate after an action. router.push() adds to history, router.replace() replaces the current entry, router.back() and router.forward() move through history.

steps.heading: usePathname & useSearchParams
steps.text: `usePathname()` returns the current path as a string — useful for highlighting active nav links. `useSearchParams()` reads the ?key=value query string. Both are Client Component hooks from `next/navigation`.

steps.heading: redirect() and notFound()
steps.text: `redirect()` from `next/navigation` navigates programmatically from Server Components, Server Functions, and Route Handlers — no need for a Client Component. `notFound()` renders the nearest `not-found.tsx` file. Both throw internally, so code after them never runs.

syntax.label: Link
syntax.note: Prefetches on hover — use instead of <a>

syntax.label: redirect()
syntax.note: Throws internally — no code runs after it

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use Link for all navigation in JSX. Use useRouter for navigation triggered by non-link interactions. Use redirect() and notFound() in Server Components and Server Actions for auth guards and missing resources.
commonMistakes: Using <a href> instead of Link — causes a full page reload and loses client-side state
Calling useRouter in a Server Component — it is a Client Component hook only
Forgetting that redirect() throws — any code after redirect() will not execute

nextjs-server-components.title: Server Components
nextjs-server-components.description: Server Components run permanently on the server — async, access databases directly, and ship zero JavaScript to the browser.

steps.heading: Always on the Server
steps.text: React Server Components run exclusively on the server and are never shipped to the browser bundle. They can be `async`, access databases and secrets directly, and read from the filesystem. Every component in the App Router is a Server Component by default — no directive needed.

steps.heading: Direct Data Access
steps.text: Server Components can call databases, read files, and fetch from internal APIs directly — no separate API endpoint required. Sensitive credentials never leave the server because the component never runs in the browser.

steps.heading: What You Cannot Do
steps.text: Server Components cannot use React state, effects, or any browser API. No `useState`, `useEffect`, `useContext`, `useRef`. No event handlers like `onClick` or `onChange`. No `window`, `document`, `localStorage`. If you need any of these, add `'use client'` or extract the interactive part into a separate Client Component.

steps.heading: Composing with Client Components
steps.text: Server Components can render Client Components as children. Pass serializable data (strings, numbers, plain objects, arrays) as props. The component tree can freely mix server and client — each component renders in the environment it belongs to.

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use Server Components for everything that fetches data, displays static content, or should not add to the browser bundle. Only switch to Client Components when you need interactivity, state, or browser APIs.
commonMistakes: Adding useState or useEffect to a Server Component — move interactive logic to a Client Component
Trying to pass a function as a prop to a Client Component — use Server Actions for server-side callbacks
Importing a heavy client-side library in a Server Component — check that it supports server environments

nextjs-client-components.title: Client Components
nextjs-client-components.description: Add 'use client' to enable hooks, event handlers, and browser APIs. Client Components are still server-rendered for the initial HTML — the directive opts into interactivity, not client-only rendering.

steps.heading: 'use client' Directive
steps.text: Add 'use client' as the very first line of a file to mark it as a Client Component. This opts the entire file — and everything it imports — into the client bundle. The directive is a boundary declaration: it tells Next.js where the server-to-client handoff happens.

steps.heading: When to Use Client Components
steps.text: Use 'use client' when your component needs: React hooks (useState, useEffect, useRef, useContext), event handlers (onClick, onChange, onSubmit), browser APIs (window, document, localStorage, navigator), or real-time updates. If none of these apply, keep it as a Server Component.

steps.heading: Hydration
steps.text: Client Components are still server-rendered for the initial HTML — 'use client' does not mean skip server rendering. Next.js renders the component on the server to produce HTML (fast initial paint), then ships the JavaScript and hydrates it on the client. Hydration requires server and client renders to produce identical output.

steps.heading: Push Boundaries to the Leaves
steps.text: The 'use client' boundary includes everything imported from that file. Placing it high in the tree makes everything below it a Client Component. Best practice: push 'use client' as far down the tree as possible, to the smallest components that actually need interactivity.

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Add 'use client' only when you need hooks, events, or browser APIs. Keep the boundary as low in the tree as possible — fetch data in Server Components, then pass it down to leaf-level Client Components for interaction.
commonMistakes: Making a component async with 'use client' — 'use client' components cannot be async
Adding 'use client' to every component as a safety measure — this defeats RSC and inflates the bundle
Assuming 'use client' means no server rendering — Client Components ARE server-rendered for the initial HTML

nextjs-suspense.title: Suspense & Streaming
nextjs-suspense.description: Stream server-rendered HTML progressively with Suspense, show skeletons while data loads, and handle errors and not-found states with dedicated boundary files.

steps.heading: loading.tsx — Instant Skeleton UI
steps.text: Add loading.tsx next to any page.tsx to automatically wrap it in a Suspense boundary. Next.js streams the layout immediately while the page fetches data — users see a skeleton instead of a blank screen. loading.tsx is a Server Component and can be a simple spinner or a full skeleton.

steps.heading: Streaming with Suspense
steps.text: For more granular control — streaming individual sections of a page — use Suspense manually. Pass a fallback prop for the skeleton. Start fetching data without await, pass the Promise to a Client Component that uses use() to suspend. Multiple Suspense boundaries on one page stream independently.

steps.heading: error.tsx and not-found.tsx
steps.text: error.tsx catches any unhandled exception thrown during rendering or data fetching in its route segment. It must be a Client Component to use the reset() callback. not-found.tsx renders when notFound() is called. Place these files at different route levels to scope which errors they catch.

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use loading.tsx for page-level loading states. Use manual Suspense for streaming multiple independent data sections on one page. Use error.tsx for graceful error recovery.
commonMistakes: Awaiting the promise before passing to a Client Component — do NOT await; pass the raw Promise so Suspense can suspend
Forgetting 'use client' on error.tsx — it needs to be a Client Component to call reset()
Using loading.tsx and manual Suspense on the same route — manual Suspense gives finer control over individual sections

nextjs-data-fetching.title: Data Fetching
nextjs-data-fetching.description: Fetch data in Server Components without useEffect. Control caching with fetch options, avoid waterfalls with parallel fetching, and revalidate on demand or on a schedule.

steps.heading: fetch() in Server Components
steps.text: Server Components can call fetch() directly — no useEffect, no loading state, no API route. Next.js extends the native fetch with caching options. The response is automatically deduped: if multiple Server Components call the same URL during one render, Next.js makes only one network request.

steps.heading: Cache Options & Revalidation
steps.text: Control how Next.js caches fetch responses with the next option. force-cache (default) caches indefinitely. no-store always fetches fresh. revalidate: 60 caches but refreshes every 60 seconds. Use revalidateTag() or revalidatePath() to manually bust the cache from a Server Action.

steps.heading: Parallel Fetching
steps.text: Sequential await calls create a waterfall — each fetch waits for the previous to finish. Use Promise.all() to fire all fetches simultaneously. This is especially important in nested layouts and pages where multiple pieces of data are needed.

steps.heading: Database Access & ORM
steps.text: Server Components can import database clients directly — no API layer needed. Use an ORM like Prisma or Drizzle to query your database with full TypeScript types. Add server-only import to any module that should never accidentally run in the browser.

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Fetch data directly in Server Components — no API routes, no useEffect. Use parallel fetching for all independent data. Use timed revalidation for data that changes occasionally, no-store for real-time data, and manual revalidation for mutation-driven updates.
commonMistakes: Sequential await for independent data — use Promise.all() to run fetches in parallel and avoid waterfalls
Using fetch inside a Client Component with useEffect — move fetches to Server Components to keep them server-side
Forgetting to check res.ok before calling res.json() — a 404 or 500 response does not throw; always check before parsing

nextjs-server-actions.title: Server Actions
nextjs-server-actions.description: Mark async functions with 'use server' to create Server Actions — call them from forms or Client Components to mutate data without building an API endpoint.

steps.heading: 'use server' Functions
steps.text: A Server Action is an async function marked with 'use server'. It runs only on the server but can be called from anywhere — a Client Component, a form action prop, or another Server Component. Next.js serializes the call over HTTP transparently. Use Server Actions for all mutations: creating, updating, deleting data.

steps.heading: Form Actions
steps.text: Pass a Server Action directly to a form's action prop — no onSubmit, no e.preventDefault(), no fetch(). The browser POSTs the FormData to the Server Action. This works even with JavaScript disabled — a progressive enhancement.

steps.heading: useActionState — Pending & Errors
steps.text: useActionState wraps a Server Action with pending/error state in a Client Component. It returns [state, action, isPending]. The action replaces the raw Server Action — pass it to the form. isPending is true while the action runs. The state holds the return value of the last call.

steps.heading: Calling Actions from Client Components
steps.text: Server Actions can be called programmatically from Client Components — not just from forms. Import the action and call it like any async function. Use useTransition to get an isPending flag without tying it to a form. Useful for delete buttons, toggle actions, and any non-form mutation.

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use Server Actions for all data mutations — create, update, delete. Prefer form actions for forms (progressive enhancement). Use useActionState for forms that need error/pending UI. Use useTransition for non-form mutations like delete buttons and toggles.
commonMistakes: Forgetting revalidatePath() or revalidateTag() after a mutation — the UI will show stale data until the next full reload
Using Server Actions for data fetching — they are for mutations only; use async Server Components for reads
Placing 'use server' in a Client Component file — Server Actions must be in separate files or inline in Server Components

nextjs-route-handlers.title: Route Handlers
nextjs-route-handlers.description: Build API endpoints with Route Handlers — HTTP GET/POST/DELETE endpoints using the standard Web Request and Response APIs.

steps.heading: Route Handler Files
steps.text: Create a route.ts file inside app/api/ to define HTTP endpoints. Export named functions for each method: GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE. The file uses the standard Web Request and Response APIs. Route Handlers can be async and can access databases and secrets just like Server Components.

steps.heading: Reading Request Data
steps.text: Route Handlers receive a standard Request object. Use request.json() for JSON bodies, request.formData() for form submissions, request.headers for headers, and new URL(request.url) to parse query parameters. Use NextRequest from next/server for extra utilities like cookies() and nextUrl.

steps.heading: Route Handlers vs Server Actions
steps.text: Use Server Actions (preferred) for mutations triggered by forms and buttons in your own UI — no API layer needed. Use Route Handlers when you need a proper HTTP API: webhooks from third parties, consumption by mobile apps or external clients, or when you need to return custom status codes and headers.

cheatSheet.whenToUse: Use Route Handlers for webhooks, public APIs consumed by external clients, and cases where you need custom HTTP status codes or headers. For all mutations from your own React UI, prefer Server Actions.
commonMistakes: Building Route Handlers for every mutation in your own UI — Server Actions are simpler and more integrated for internal mutations
Forgetting to handle the error case with a proper status code — always return appropriate HTTP status codes
Mixing route.ts and page.tsx in the same folder — Next.js will throw an error; API routes and page routes must be in separate folders

react-router-advanced.title: React Router as a Framework
react-router-advanced.description: Go beyond the library — use React Router v7's framework mode with route modules, loaders, and actions for full-stack data handling

steps.heading: Library vs Framework mode
steps.text: React Router started as a client-side routing library. In v7, it can also run as a full framework — handling file-based routes, server-side rendering, and data loading out of the box. In library mode you configure BrowserRouter yourself; in framework mode you scaffold with create-react-router and React Router owns the entry point.

steps.heading: routes.js config
steps.text: Framework mode uses a routes.js file to declare your route tree. The index() helper declares the index route; route() declares named routes with a URL pattern and a file path to the route module.

steps.heading: Route modules
steps.text: A route module is a file that exports named functions React Router understands: default (the component), loader/clientLoader (data fetching), action/clientAction (mutations), and ErrorBoundary (error UI). React Router wires these automatically — no manual prop passing needed.

steps.heading: clientLoader — CSR data loading
steps.text: clientLoader fetches data in the browser before the component renders. It receives { params } for URL parameters. Export HydrateFallback to show a loading UI while it runs on the first visit.

steps.heading: loader — SSR data loading
steps.text: loader runs on the server — before the HTML is sent to the browser. It can safely access databases or server-only APIs without exposing them to the client bundle. The component receives the same loaderData prop — no changes needed.

steps.heading: Form & actions
steps.text: clientAction and action handle form mutations. React Router's Form component serialises the submission and calls the matching action. The result is available as actionData. After the action runs, loaders automatically revalidate — no manual refetch.

steps.heading: Rendering strategies
steps.text: React Router v7 supports three rendering strategies in react-router.config.js. CSR (SPA mode) renders everything in the browser; SSR renders HTML on every server request; SSG pre-renders pages at build time. Your choice determines when loaders run.

react-data-fetching.title: Data Fetching Strategies
react-data-fetching.description: Understand the fetch-on-render waterfall and how render-as-you-fetch with Suspense eliminates it

steps.heading: The waterfall problem
steps.text: When a parent component fetches data and only renders a child after its data arrives, and that child fetches its own data, you get a waterfall — each request waits for the previous one. Total wait time equals the sum of all requests.

steps.heading: Fetch-on-render (useEffect pattern)
steps.text: The classic pattern puts fetching inside useEffect. The component renders first with a spinner, then the effect fires, then state updates cause a re-render with data. It forces sequential rendering: a child cannot start fetching until its parent has finished and rendered it.

steps.heading: Render-as-you-fetch
steps.text: Render-as-you-fetch starts all fetches before the component tree renders — then renders components that suspend until their data arrives. Fetches run in parallel so total time equals the slowest single request, not the sum.

steps.heading: Suspense + use()
steps.text: React's use() hook unwraps a promise inside a component. If the promise is not resolved yet, the component suspends and React shows the nearest Suspense fallback. Once resolved, the component renders with the data. ErrorBoundary catches rejections.

steps.heading: Loaders as the solution
steps.text: The cleanest render-as-you-fetch implementation delegates fetching to your router. React Router's loader runs the moment a navigation starts — before any component renders — and returns the data the route needs. No useEffect, no manual promise management.

react-mutations.title: Data Mutations
react-mutations.description: Send data back to the server — from classic onSubmit patterns to React 19 form actions and useFormStatus

steps.heading: onSubmit + preventDefault
steps.text: The classic React form pattern intercepts the native submit event, calls preventDefault() to stop the page from reloading, then manually sends the data with fetch. You manage loading, error, and success state yourself with useState.

steps.heading: Form actions (React 19)
steps.text: React 19 introduces the action prop on form elements. Pass an async function directly — React calls it with a FormData object when the form submits. No onSubmit, no preventDefault needed.

steps.heading: FormData
steps.text: FormData is the browser's built-in way to collect form field values. Each input's name attribute becomes a key. formData.get() retrieves a single value; formData.getAll() retrieves multiple values for checkboxes and multi-select inputs.

steps.heading: useFormStatus — pending state
steps.text: useFormStatus() from react-dom reads the pending state of the nearest parent form action. Use it inside a submit button component to disable the button while the action runs. The component must be inside the form — it reads context, not props.

steps.heading: Error boundaries for forms
steps.text: If a form action throws, wrapping the form in an ErrorBoundary catches the error and renders a fallback UI. The fallback receives resetErrorBoundary so the user can retry. Best for unexpected server failures — not per-field validation.

steps.heading: useActionState
steps.text: useActionState(action, initialState) wraps a form action and returns the latest result as state — perfect for per-field validation errors. Your action receives (previousState, formData) and returns the new state with validation errors or a success flag.

react-tanstack-query.title: TanStack Query
react-tanstack-query.description: Add smart client-side caching to your React app — stale-while-revalidate, background refetch, and mutations without the boilerplate

steps.heading: The caching problem
steps.text: Every time a user navigates back to a route, a plain useEffect fetch fires again — even if the data has not changed. Client-side caching stores fetch results keyed by query, so subsequent visits are instant and the network is only hit when data is stale.

steps.heading: Stale vs fresh data
steps.text: TanStack Query tracks whether cached data is fresh (recently fetched — serve as-is) or stale (old enough that a background refetch should happen). staleTime controls the window — 0 means always stale, Infinity means never auto-refetch. Default is 0.

steps.heading: useQuery
steps.text: useQuery takes a queryKey and a queryFn. queryKey is an array that uniquely identifies the query — include all variables the fetch depends on. queryFn is the async function. Returns data, isLoading, isError, and error.

steps.heading: Background refetch
steps.text: When a stale query mounts again, TanStack Query shows the cached data immediately and refetches in the background. If new data arrives, the UI updates automatically. Users see content instantly on return visits — no full spinner.

steps.heading: useMutation
steps.text: useMutation handles write operations. Call mutate with your variables to trigger it. Use onSuccess to invalidate related queries — queryClient.invalidateQueries() marks matching queries stale and triggers a refetch, keeping the UI in sync after mutations.

steps.heading: React Router loader integration
steps.text: React Router loaders re-run on every navigation by default — even when the data has not changed. You can integrate TanStack Query with loaders using `queryClient.ensureQueryData()`: the loader populates the cache, and subsequent visits are served instantly without a network round-trip.
steps.expandedText:
📜 Using `ensureQueryData` inside a router loader is a **cache-first** data-loading strategy: if fresh data is already in the cache (within `staleTime`), the loader returns immediately with no network request — navigation is instant. If data is stale or absent, the loader fetches it before the component mounts. The component then calls `useQuery` with the same key to subscribe to the cache, receiving the pre-loaded data on its first render with no loading state.

📜 This pattern combines the best of both worlds: **router loaders** prevent the waterfall and ensure data is available before render; **TanStack Query** provides caching, background refetching, and real-time updates after the initial load. The component has a live subscription to the cache — if data is invalidated after mount (by a mutation elsewhere), the component automatically re-renders with fresh data. Neither the loader nor the component needs to be modified to gain this behaviour.

analogy.title: Step 5 — Loader integration: data on the platform before boarding
text: Arriving at the platform to find your luggage already loaded. You board and everything is ready.
Prefetch in the loader with `queryClient.prefetchQuery(...)`. When the component mounts, the cache is warm — `useQuery` returns data instantly.

react-useref.title: useRef & Refs
react-useref.description: Access DOM nodes and store mutable values without triggering re-renders

steps.heading: A ref is a mutable box
steps.text: `useRef` returns a plain JavaScript object `{ current: initialValue }`. Unlike `useState`, changing `ref.current` never triggers a re-render — React is completely unaware of the change. A ref is a mutable escape hatch: it persists across renders just like state, but mutations are invisible to the render cycle.

steps.heading: DOM access
steps.text: The most common use for refs is accessing DOM elements directly — to focus an input, measure dimensions, or integrate third-party libraries that need a real DOM node. Attach the ref via the `ref` prop on any JSX element; after mount, `ref.current` points to the live DOM node.

steps.heading: Storing mutable values
steps.text: Refs are perfect for values that need to persist across renders but should NOT trigger a re-render when changed: timer IDs, WebSocket connections, previous prop values. Storing these in state causes unnecessary re-renders; storing them in regular variables resets them on every render.

steps.heading: Avoiding stale closures
steps.text: Event handlers close over state values at the time they are created. If state updates between creation and execution, the captured value is stale. Storing the latest value in a ref and reading `ref.current` inside the callback always gives the current value — refs are not captured stale by closures.

steps.heading: forwardRef — passing refs to children
steps.text: By default, the `ref` prop does not pass through custom components — only HTML elements accept refs directly. `forwardRef` wraps a component to forward the ref from the parent to a specific element inside it. In React 19, `ref` is available as a regular prop and `forwardRef` is no longer needed.

react-custom-hooks.title: Custom Hooks
react-custom-hooks.description: Extract and reuse stateful logic across components without changing their structure

steps.heading: The duplication problem
steps.text: When two components need the same stateful logic — fetching data, reading localStorage, tracking window size — the only options without custom hooks are: duplicate the code, or lift it into a parent. Custom hooks are a third option: extract the hook logic into a separate function and call it from any component.

steps.heading: Rules of custom hooks
steps.text: A custom hook is a function whose name starts with `use`. That prefix tells React and the linter to apply the Rules of Hooks. Custom hooks can call `useState`, `useEffect`, or other custom hooks — they compose naturally. Each call to a custom hook gets its own isolated state.

steps.heading: useLocalStorage
steps.text: `useLocalStorage` wraps `useState` and syncs the value to `localStorage`. On mount it reads the stored value; on change it writes back. The component treats it exactly like `useState` — same destructuring, same setter. The persistence is an invisible implementation detail.

steps.heading: useDebounce
steps.text: `useDebounce` delays updating a value until the user has stopped changing it for a given duration. It uses `useEffect` with a `setTimeout` cleanup: start a timer on every value change, clear the previous timer. When the value stops changing, the timer fires and updates the debounced value.

steps.heading: Composing hooks
steps.text: Custom hooks compose — a hook can call other custom hooks. `useFetch` might call `useDebounce` for the query and `useState` for the result. The consumer gets a clean `{ data, loading, error }` interface. Complexity is buried in the implementation, not scattered across components.

nextjs-error-handling.title: Error Handling
nextjs-error-handling.description: Catch render errors and missing pages with error.tsx, global-error.tsx, and not-found.tsx

steps.heading: Unhandled errors crash the page
steps.text: Without error boundaries, a JavaScript error during rendering propagates up the component tree and crashes the entire page. React's `ErrorBoundary` class component can catch these errors, but writing one manually is verbose. Next.js App Router provides file-based error boundaries as a first-class feature.

steps.heading: error.tsx — route segment boundary
steps.text: Create `error.tsx` inside any route folder and Next.js automatically wraps that segment in a React error boundary. When `page.tsx` throws, `error.tsx` renders instead — with the surrounding layout still intact. The component receives `{ error, reset }` props: `error` for the caught error, `reset` to retry rendering the segment. Must be a Client Component.

steps.heading: not-found.tsx + notFound()
steps.text: For 404-style errors — when a resource simply does not exist — use the `notFound()` function inside a Server Component. It throws a special error that Next.js catches and renders the nearest `not-found.tsx` file. A root-level `app/not-found.tsx` provides the site-wide 404 page.

steps.heading: global-error.tsx
steps.text: `global-error.tsx` at the root `app/` level catches errors thrown by the root `layout.tsx` — the one place `error.tsx` cannot protect. It replaces the entire HTML output including `<html>` and `<body>`, so it must provide those tags itself. Must be a Client Component.

steps.heading: The complete error hierarchy
steps.text: Next.js error files form a waterfall: `global-error.tsx` wraps everything; segment-level `error.tsx` files wrap individual pages; `not-found.tsx` handles missing resources. Outer boundaries catch what inner ones miss. A well-structured app has a root `not-found.tsx`, a root `error.tsx`, and specific boundaries around unstable data-fetching pages.
